EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-pressure system which had been moving slowly off the lee side of the Rocky Mountains had finally progressed into central Nebraska. This left western Iowa in the warm sector as a warm front moved north into the area. The SPC issued an Enhanced Risk for severe weather with a 30% hatched for severe hail. The severe weather mostly stayed west of the Missouri River. In Iowa, there was one report for a severe wind gust. The most impactful weather was a line of training storms that lead to flash flooding in Harrison County that caused significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 4.4 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level wave moving through southwest flow aloft, acting on an environment with 1000 to 1500 J/kg of MUCAPE in the vicinity of a surface trough, resulted in scattered thunderstorms across far southeast South Dakota, far northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota during the evening of April 19th. The strongest storms produced severe hail ranging from 1.0 to 1.5 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail 1.0 inch in diameter fell 5 miles west-northwest of Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread significant damaging wind storm tracked across nearly all of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa on the morning of July 12. These storms produced widespread winds of 60 to 80 mph, with embedded stronger downbursts with wind speeds up to 100 mph. The strongest measured wind gust was 82 mph in Dodge County NE, but some of the high-end straight line wind damage supports embedded wind speeds up to 100 mph.||This storm also produced a few embedded tornadoes. Some of the tornado tracks were only identified by narrow swaths of damage through mature cornfields, with minimal damage otherwise and peak wind speeds similar to the nearby downburst winds. All but one of the tornadoes was preceded by a Wireless Emergency Alert. There were 0 reported injuries or fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a semi blown over and part of roof blown off a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated storms developed in a deep shear environment and produced severe hail ranging between 1 to 1.5 inches during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 1-inch hail via mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across western Iowa which had observed unseasonably warm temperatures in the past couple of days. A flash flood was also reported in Pottawattamie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public personal weather station recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms tracked across southeast Iowa during the mid-afternoon into early evening of June 24, producing large hail up to golf ball size in Van Buren County and tennis ball size hail near Ottumwa.||More organized clusters of severe storms tracked through Dubuque into far northwest IL during the evening and late night hours of June 24.  Another storm cluster moved through the Quad Cities, producing wind gusts to 70 mph at the Davenport ASOS and damage to trees and power poles.  An EF-0 tornado also tracked east along and north of I-80 near the Davenport Airport, causing sporadic minor tree and structure damage.  This storm impacted the Quad City Airshow grounds, while no visitors were present.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed near Overton Road and 286th Street in Harrison County Iowa. The tornado moved rapidly to the east where it made a direct impact on a home. At this location, the home roof was removed, windows broken, siding ruined, numerous trees snapped, a shed was demolished, and numerous items were thrown up to a half mile downstream. The convergent damage pattern at this location, eyewitness reports of wind direction, along with the 80-yard-wide path of more intense damage gives great confidence that this was a tornado. Winds at this location were estimated at 120 mph based on damage. The tornado moved quickly southeast, causing substantial tree damage with snapped trunks being common along the continued 50 to 100 yard wide track. The tornado moved through neighborhood of acreages along Quentin trail where it snapped many trees but moved in between the homes. The track then became more intermittent for a couple miles before it partially removed the roof of a home along 296th Street and caused additional narrow tree damage for the next two miles. The final damage point that was pretty clearly tornado damage was near 305th and Upton where 3 grain bins were crumpled and blown downstream and a machine shed sustained substantial damage. Beyond this point, the tree damage was sporadic and more evident of the larger scale wind field. Should also note that to the west of this tornado track, there was a debris signature evident on radar, but the damage was much more consistent with a focused downburst or series of downbursts that moved up from the Missouri Valley into the Loess Hills and may have helped to loft light leafy debris. This downburst likely led to the development of the eventual tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17. An initial band of precipitation developed during the early morning hours of March 16 across northwest Iowa in the form of light rain or light freezing rain. However, a sharp fall in temperatures by midday and early afternoon forced a change to a few hours of snowfall accompanied by increasing northerly wind. Snow cover prior to the event was limited to areas north of Highway 18, where impacts from snow and blowing snow with winds of 45 to 55 mph were greatest during the afternoon of March 16, and again briefly on Friday, March 17 when reinvigorated winds brought very brief visibility of a quarter mile or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain resulted in a glaze of icing at the COOP site at Spencer. Northerly wind increased by the afternoon with gusts as high as 60 mph at Automated Surface Observing System site KSPW near Spencer.

All but one of the tornadoes was preceded by a Wireless Emergency Alert. There were 0 reported injuries or fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed just north of 1150th street and east of Highway 191 where it destroyed two barns and damaged others. The tornado track continued east with evidence of convergent flow pattern within the debris field and the cornfield as it tracked to the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt during late March into early April, as well as some added rainfall, resulted in rises on some small streams and creeks across the region. Some of these rises pushed toward or into minor flood stage for a short period, but overall impacts were limited mainly to agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River near Milford, IA rose above flood stage on the evening of April 1st before cresting around 1.59 feet above flood stage (13.59 feet) on April 5th. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of April 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed across portions of eastern Iowa overnight on the 9th and into the 10th, with visibilities to a quarter mile at times through the mid morning. Freezing fog resulted in slippery pavement. Early in the morning, a crash resulted on Interstate 80 due to ice covered roads. This incident led to two fatalities and several injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa State Patrol responded to a crash due to ice-covered roads, caused by overnight freezing fog and drizzle. A semi truck driver lost control of their vehicle and jackknifed on Interstate 80, which led to two fatalities and several injuries. Total number of injuries are unknown. 16 total cars were involved in the pile-up that morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system spun northeast across Iowa on January 16, 2023, drawing mild temperatures northward into the area and sparking some showers and thunderstorms over portions of eastern Iowa.  Temperatures increased into the upper 40s to low 60s in parts of east central and southeast Iowa by early afternoon.  One storm ended up becoming severe, producing two tornadoes between 2 PM and 3 PM CST.   The first tornado touched down just northeast of Williamsburg, IA and the second one briefly touched down north of Ely, IA.  Some large hail up to the size of quarters was reported in Homestead, IA from this storm at 2:24 PM.  Several other areas that the storm tracked over reported hail covering the ground. These were the first tornadoes reported in Iowa in the month of January since January 24, 1967.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing warm advection brought a period of freezing rain to northwest Iowa on January 2, which was reinvigorated by convection early on January 3. The most significant ice accumulations in excess of one-quarter inch occurred toward Highway 71, with lesser amounts to the west. Impacts were lessened by air temperatures which climbed to near or above freezing after the initial push of freezing rain. Colder air eventually resulted in a changeover to snow during January 3, with a band of heavy snowfall across far northern portions of northwest Iowa and rates up to 1 to 2 inches per hour during the early morning and midday hours. Roads in far northwest Iowa were closed or impassable during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain resulted in ice accumulations of almost one-half inch at Spencer. Cooler air changed precipitation to snow during the afternoon of January 3, which resulted in an accumulation of 3.7 inches at Spencer on top of the prior ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent fog from the morning into the early afternoon of January 11 resulted in long periods of visibility less than one-quarter mile, as well as deposition of frost on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severely limited visibility and frosty roadways contributed to a two-vehicle accident at the intersection of U.S. Highway 59 and 20 just south of Holstein. An indirect fatality occurred to the driver of a car when it was hit broadside by a semi-truck upon entering the roadway after a stop at the intersection.

Colder air eventually resulted in a changeover to snow during January 3, with a band of heavy snowfall across far northern portions of northwest Iowa and rates up to 1 to 2 inches per hour during the early morning and midday hours. Roads in far northwest Iowa were closed or impassable during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a period of overnight and early morning freezing rain with up to a half inch of ice accumulation, heavy snowfall developed at times during the late morning and early afternoon of January 3 with a two-day total of 8.4 inches at Spirit Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed just northeast of Williamsburg, and south of Interstate 80. The tornado blew over a semi tractor trailer on I-80, then continued to the northeast. The tornado caused minor damage to a cattle shelter, and tree damage at a farmstead, before lifting near U.S. Highway 151. The tornado was on the ground for roughly 8 minutes, along a path estimated at 4.7 miles. There were no injuries. Assistance from Iowa County Emergency Management in completing the survey is much appreciated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Charles City (Floyd County) driver died in a single motor vehicle accident after losing control on a rural road east of Charles City. Rural roads across portions of Floyd County remained slippery after a snow event that occurred from January 18th into the 19th. Reports indicate the driver lost control on a section of road that was snow and ice covered and was partially thrown from the vehicle when it crashed into a creek bank.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer in Charles City reported 9.1 inches of snow had fallen from the evening of January 18th into the early morning hours of January 19th. A fatal single vehicle accident occurred east of Charles City on January 23rd, where roads remained snow and ice covered following the snowfall on January 18th and 19th. The driver lost control and was partially thrown from the vehicle when it crashed into a creek bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. After initially warm thermal profiles resulted in mainly rain or a brief mix, infiltration of colder air and the change to light snow resulted in minor accumulations mainly to the north and west. However, northwest Iowa did not escape strong northwest winds which reached a gusty 40 to 60 mph, and many areas found considerable drifting of earlier snowfall along and north of Iowa Highway 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 30 to 35 mph for several hours at Iowa State Mesonet site DONI4 near Doon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted out of the southwestern U.S. and closed off moving across Iowa by early on January 19. A band of snow developed within the leading warm advection zone from northern Nebraska to northern Iowa by late afternoon on January 18, and with unstable conditions near the frontal axis, the band evolved into a more narrow zone of intense snowfall into the overnight hours. Dry air poised to the north created a sharp gradient to snowfall on the north side of the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 8.5 inches at Sioux Rapids and 6.5 inches at Storm Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture out of the Gulf was transported all the way to Iowa ahead of a deep, negatively tilted trough across the Rockies. Across Iowa, this resulted in a mixed precipitation event across the state with over an inch of rain across central to southern Iowa. Into northern Iowa the thermal profile cooled and mixed precipitation was reported, including snow, graupel, and freezing rain. Within an axis of colder air, freezing rain accumulated, especially on vegetation and elevated surfaces. Light snow accumulation of up to two inches and ice formed, glazing up to nearly half an inch was across parts of northwest Iowa. Temperatures warmed quickly after sunrise on the 3rd, helping to mitigate impacts from the wintry precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow followed by nearly a half inch of ice accumulation.

Increasing warm advection brought a period of freezing rain to northwest Iowa on January 2, which was reinvigorated by convection early on January 3. The most significant ice accumulations in excess of one-quarter inch occurred toward Highway 71, with lesser amounts to the west. Impacts were lessened by air temperatures which climbed to near or above freezing after the initial push of freezing rain. Colder air eventually resulted in a changeover to snow during January 3, with a band of heavy snowfall across far northern portions of northwest Iowa and rates up to 1 to 2 inches per hour during the early morning and midday hours. Roads in far northwest Iowa were closed or impassable during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain with a light glaze of ice accumulation, heavy snowfall developed at times during the morning of January 3 with a two-day total of 12.0 inches at Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted out of the southwestern U.S. and closed off moving across Iowa by early on January 19. A band of snow developed within the leading warm advection zone from northern Nebraska to northern Iowa by late afternoon on January 18, and with unstable conditions near the frontal axis, the band evolved into a more narrow zone of intense snowfall into the overnight hours. Dry air poised to the north created a sharp gradient to snowfall on the north side of the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 6.3 inches at Cherokee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm slammed the Mid-Mississippi River Valley during the weekend of January 28th and 29th. Snowfall accumulations of 4 to 6 inches grazed select counties in northeast Iowa. Highest total snowfall accumulations were 7.5 inches near Fayette (Fayette County), 7.4 inches near Fredericksburg (Chickasaw County), and 7.1 inches near Elkader (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 6 inches of snow fell in select areas of Clayton County. The highest reported total was 7.1 inches near Elkader.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across northwest Iowa on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph. Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23. During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls. Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around an inch near Highway 20 to around 10 inches near the Minnesota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 8.0 inches at Larchwood and Rock Rapids. Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 45 mph at Sibley and 39 mph at Luverne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With colder air entrenched across the northern tier of the U.S., a jet streak dropped southeast out of Canada and a strong zone of frontogenetic forcing along the boundary of colder air resulted in a band of heavy snowfall, with brief rates up to 1 to 2 inches per hour through the Missouri River corridor into northwest Iowa. Winds were not excessive with the system, with no significant blowing and drifting impacts. Snowfall generally reached 6 to 12 inches along the Missouri River, but dry air poised to the north rapidly dropped amounts to an inch or less north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.0 inches in George.  The roof of the community center in Inwood collapsed from the accumulated weight of snowfall over the past month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass with temperatures of 10 to 25 degrees below zero spread across northeast Iowa at the end of January. These temperatures combined with wind speeds of 10 to 15 mph to produce dangerously cold wind chills nearing 40 below zero during the morning of hours January 31st. The coldest wind chill recorded was 40 below zero at the Charles City (Floyd County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures of 35 to 40 below zero occurred across Floyd County during the early morning of January 31st. The coldest wind chill of 40 below occurred at the automated weather observing equipment at Charles City Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passage on February 15 resulted in a temperature drop from the 40s and 50s down into the 10s and 20s. A low pressure system tracked across the Southern Plains initiating precipitation. The heaviest precipitation was on the north side of the low. In southeast Nebraska, there was a wide swath of 6 to 8 inches of snow with a peak of 8.2 inches in Mills County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer reported 6.5 inches of snowfall.

With colder air entrenched across the northern tier of the U.S., a jet streak dropped southeast out of Canada and a strong zone of frontogenetic forcing along the boundary of colder air resulted in a band of heavy snowfall, with brief rates up to 1 to 2 inches per hour through the Missouri River corridor into northwest Iowa. Winds were not excessive with the system, with no significant blowing and drifting impacts. Snowfall generally reached 6 to 12 inches along the Missouri River, but dry air poised to the north rapidly dropped amounts to an inch or less north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 6.0 inches 2 miles southwest of Paullina and 4.7 inches at Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low underwent lee cyclogenesis east of the Rockies, lifting towards Iowa and bringing a band of snow across central to northern Iowa. Snow amounts were in the four to six inch range, but were accompanied by wind gusts of 40 to 47 mph throughout the event thanks to a tight pressure gradient across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Combination of at least 4 inches of snow and wind gusts over 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across northwest Iowa on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph. Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23. During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls. Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around an inch near Highway 20 to around 10 inches near the Minnesota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 5.1 inches at Primghar and 5.0 inches at Sheldon.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 44 mph at Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passage on February 15 resulted in a temperature drop from the 40s and 50s down into the 10s and 20s. A low pressure system tracked across the Southern Plains initiating precipitation. The heaviest precipitation was on the north side of the low. In southeast Nebraska, there was a wide swath of 6 to 8 inches of snow with a peak of 8.2 inches in Mills County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported 8-inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. After initially warm thermal profiles resulted in mainly rain or a brief mix, infiltration of colder air and the change to light snow resulted in minor accumulations mainly to the north and west. However, northwest Iowa did not escape strong northwest winds which reached a gusty 40 to 60 mph, and many areas found considerable drifting of earlier snowfall along and north of Iowa Highway 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 30 to 39 mph, with gusts as high as 47 mph at personal weather station FW7399 at Terril.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deeper than average snow pack from northern Minnesota and northern Wisconsin underwent a rapid melt through much of April from unseasonably warm temperatures and multiple rain events. This resulted in increased water levels and significant flooding for the entire length of the local Mississippi River, with most sites climbing to their third to fifth highest crests on record. In response to the widespread flooding, the Governor of Iowa issued a Proclamation of Disaster Emergency for counties along the Mississippi River. Initial flood warnings were issued in mid April with peak stages realized towards late April. The highest above flood stage was Guttenberg Dam at 6.91 ft over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of rainfall and melting snow pushed the Mississippi River at Guttenberg into flood stage on April 18th. The crest of 20.98 ft on April 28th was 5.98 ft over flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intermittent light snow from  Saturday November 25th through Sunday November 26th resulted in slippery road conditions for the busy Thanksgiving holiday travel weekend. Law enforcement and broadcast media reported widespread vehicle incidents through the weekend. While snowfall rates remained under 0.1 inches per hour for most of the weekend, a scattered increase to 0.25 inches per hour Sunday evening temporarily reduced visibilities in spots. The longer duration resulted in a widespread 1.0 to 2.0 inches of overall accumulations by Sunday night. Highest snowfall reports were 2.8 inches near Strawberry Point (Clayton County) and 2.0 inches near Harpers Ferry (Allamakee County), Elkader (Clayton County), and the Town of Fayette (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers reported 1.3 to 2.0 inches of snowfall across Allamakee County with the highest amount reported near Harpers Ferry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and rain moved across northeast Iowa from the  afternoon of February 21st into the morning of the 23rd. Light snow accumulations eventually changed over to rain and then freezing rain for Fayette and Clayton Counties. Ice accumulations of a quarter of an inch were reported in Oelwein (Fayette County) and north of Buena Vista (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell over the southern parts of Clayton County during the early morning hours of February 23rd. Public reports indicated a quarter inch of ice accumulation occurred on power lines across main street in Buena Vista.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the combination of rainfall and melting snow caused some northeast Iowa river flooding to occur along the Turkey and Volga Rivers in late February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the combination of rainfall and melting snow pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Elkader. The river crested almost two feet above the flood stage at 13.77 feet on the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the combination of rainfall and melting snow caused some northeast Iowa river flooding to occur along the Turkey and Volga Rivers in late February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the combination of rainfall and melting snow pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Garber. The river crested almost a foot and a half above the flood stage at 18.35 feet on the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the combination of rainfall and melting snow caused some northeast Iowa river flooding to occur along the Turkey and Volga Rivers in late February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the combination of rainfall and snow melt pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Eldorado. The river had two crests, with the second the highest. This crest was a little over a half foot above the flood stage at 13.65 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and unseasonably warm system brought showers and thunderstorms to the state. Over an inch of rain fell across much of Iowa. In northern Iowa, this was on top of a snowpack of two to four inches. The quick rain on top of frozen group resulted in runoff sufficient to send the Winnebago River at Mason City into minor river flooding. The river exceeded minor flood stage and quickly crested the morning of February 27th before falling back below flood stage the morning of February 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River entered flood state at 430am LST on the 27th of February, crested at 11.24 ft at 630am LST on February 27th, and fell below flood stage at 430am LST on February 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system impacted northeast Iowa from the evening of Saturday, April 15th into Sunday night, April 16th. Initially, rain and thunderstorms spread through northern Iowa before switching over to snow during the morning hours of Sunday, April 16th. The mix of rain and snow resulted in variable snowfall amounts from 0 inches across Floyd, Howard, and Mitchell counties to 8 inches near Elkader (Clayton County) and 6.8 near Churchtown (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer public observers reported 4 to 6 inches of snowfall in Winneshiek County. The highest reported total of 7.5 inches was recorded near Decorah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level short-wave and mid-level front produced areas of light to moderate snow across far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska during the late evening of March 22nd into the early overnight hours of March 23rd. Stronger frontal forcing resulted in snow accumulations from 2 to 6 inches. In addition, thundersnow and periods of graupel were observed at times. Winds were relatively weak with this system, such that overall impacts were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Primghar measured 2.0 inches of snow and the COOP observer in Sheldon measured 2.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17. An initial band of precipitation developed during the early morning hours of March 16 across northwest Iowa in the form of light rain or light freezing rain. However, a sharp fall in temperatures by midday and early afternoon forced a change to a few hours of snowfall accompanied by increasing northerly wind. Snow cover prior to the event was limited to areas north of Highway 18, where impacts from snow and blowing snow with winds of 45 to 55 mph were greatest during the afternoon of March 16, and again briefly on Friday, March 17 when reinvigorated winds brought very brief visibility of a quarter mile or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain resulted in a glaze of icing, prior to a period of snowfall that accumulated to 0.5 inches at a CoCoRaHS site near Spirit Lake. Northerly wind increased by the afternoon of March 16 with gusts as high as 46 mph at personal weather stations EW9051 near Spirit Lake and FW7399 at Terril, resulting in brief visibility reduction to around one-half to one mile. After winds decreased overnight and early morning of March 17 with minimal impacts, winds increased again during the later morning and afternoon with frequent convective snow showers that briefly reduced visibility at times to one mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt during late March resulted in rises on some small streams and creeks across the region. Some of these rises pushed toward or into minor flood stage for a short period, but overall impacts were limited mainly to agricultural land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system brought severe weather to parts of northeast Iowa during the late afternoon of Friday, March 31, 2023. Reports of 1.0 to 1.25 inch hail were reported in Mitchell and Floyd Counties. The largest hail reported was half dollar in Stacyville (Mitchell County) and Colwell (Floyd County). An EF0 tornado entered rural Clayton County out of Delaware County near Edgewood and dissipated shortly thereafter, resulting in minimal damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed in Delaware County, crossed into Clayton County between Edgewood and Colesburg, and crossed Horseshoe Road before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across northwest Iowa on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph. Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23. During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls. Travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied from around an inch near Highway 20 to around 10 inches near the Minnesota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 7.5 inches at Rock Valley and 7.0 inches in Sioux Center. Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 40 mph near Maurice and 36 mph at Hawarden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. After initially warm thermal profiles resulted in mainly rain or a brief mix, infiltration of colder air and the change to light snow resulted in minor accumulations mainly to the north and west. However, northwest Iowa did not escape strong northwest winds which reached a gusty 40 to 60 mph, and many areas found considerable drifting of earlier snowfall along and north of Iowa Highway 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 30 to 39 mph, with gusts as high as 49 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site KSHL at Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning storms associated with a weakening low resulted in one stronger cell in central Iowa producing quarter sized hail before dawn. Later in the day, a deepening low across the central plains lifted into Iowa with storms developing along a dry line in the evening. These storms produced hail up to baseball sized, along with a brief tornado remained rural and did not cause any damage. Additional convection was reinvigorated overnight thanks to the low level et, but remained sub severe. Areas that received multiple rounds of storms saw two to three inches of much needed rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early to mid April in Iowa was marked by exceptionally dry, warm, and breezy conditions. This resulted in several days of Red Flag Warning issuances across the area when relative humidity dropped under 25 percent, winds gusted over 30 mph, and much above average temperatures climbed into the low 80s. This resulted in a number of fires that became uncontrolled through the first half of the month. On April 13th, one fire resulted in an individual becoming severely burned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person conducting a controlled burn was severely injured when the fire became uncontrolled. The fire quickly spread and engulfed the individual who was using a tractor to stop further spread of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough across the Rockies underwent lee cyclogenesis, pulling a warm front north into Iowa in the afternoon. While a few storms developed along the warm front, most development was along the later cold front that moved across the area in the evening. These storms were frequent hail producers, with many reports of baseball sized hail or larger. A tornado also occurred which resulted in damage to a few homes, out buildings, and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time estimated by radar.

While a few storms developed along the warm front, most development was along the later cold front that moved across the area in the evening. These storms were frequent hail producers, with many reports of baseball sized hail or larger. A tornado also occurred which resulted in damage to a few homes, out buildings, and trees.

Early morning storms associated with a weakening low resulted in one stronger cell in central Iowa producing quarter sized hail before dawn. Later in the day, a deepening low across the central plains lifted into Iowa with storms developing along a dry line in the evening. These storms produced hail up to baseball sized, along with a brief tornado remained rural and did not cause any damage. Additional convection was reinvigorated overnight thanks to the low level et, but remained sub severe. Areas that received multiple rounds of storms saw two to three inches of much needed rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via mping.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began in eastern Warren county east of Liberty Center.  It tracked northeast for approximately 15 miles,  ending in western Marion County just south of Pleasantville. Based on eyewitness accounts and video evidence, its likely this tornado  was intermittent, and not a fully continuous path. This portions of the path is in Warren county with a separate entry for Marion county portion of the path.  ||Damage along the tornado path occurred to several homes, outbuildings, and farm equipment.  The most intense damage occurred to a home and garage which suffered significant damage to exterior walls and roofing structure.  However, the home appeared to suffer a garage door failure  and broken windows, which allowed the structure to pressurize and blow outward.  Additionally, minor to moderate tree and shelter belt damage, along with several downed power poles occurred along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 knot gust measured by the ASOS at the Cedar Rapids Airport.

Additional convection was reinvigorated overnight thanks to the low level et, but remained sub severe. Areas that received multiple rounds of storms saw two to three inches of much needed rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant car damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection percolated along a warm front across the area through the morning. By afternoon, storms re-strengthened as as daytime heating helped enhance instability. Despite good instability with MLCAPE over nearing 2000 J/kg, lapse rates were modest and speed shear was weak. Despite this, directional shear was good and when combined with instability for brief stretching, allowed for a number of weak and short-lived tornadoes to develop. While 13 tornadoes occurred, they were confined to two small areas in Iowa where storms trained, resulting in several storms over the same locations. In addition, the training storms made for pockets of heavy rain with three to four inches or more of rain. This did result in flash flooding in Pocahontas County with impacts to homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado reported by storm chaser southwest of Fonda. No damage indicators were impacted, therefore the EFU rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave passed across the area, coincident with a low level plume of moisture. Moderate instability was in place with CAPE around 1500 J/kg but very weak flow and shear. While this meant that storms generally remained sub severe, the storms were also slow moving. One storm that set up over Fort Doge resulted in heavy rain with over three inches observed in one hour. Flooding occurred throughout the town with several road closures and a few stranded vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail. Reports of tree limbs of unknown size down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough lifted of of the four corners region and into the midwest, initially pulling a frontogenetical band of brief heavy snow across southern Iowa on the night of February 15th. Behind this, isentropic lift with deep saturation and lift through the dendritic growth zone allowed for heavy snow rates through the day on February 16th. By later afternoon into early evening snow began to taper off from west to east, leaving behind a wide swath of seven to nine inches of snow across southern Iowa, generally along and south of the interstate 80 corridor.

A winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow, and some mixed precipitation, to much of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri on February 16th, 2023.||Snow began during the early morning across much of east central Iowa, with several locations (especially around Iowa City) seeing snow amounts around 4-6 inches around 6:00 AM. Light snow continued throughout the morning before the arrival of a stronger band of snow by early afternoon. This band impacted the Cedar Rapids / Iowa City metros during the early-mid afternoon before reaching the Mississippi River / Quad Cities metro by late afternoon and early evening, which led to treacherous travel conditions for the evening commute.||Snowfall totals ranged from around 2-6 inches in the Quad Cities / Dubuque metros, to around 7-12 inches in the Cedar Rapids / Iowa City metros. Lesser snow amounts were observed across southeast Iowa due to sleet mixing in with snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, Iowa DOT, and the public reported snowfall of 5 to 7 inches falling over Dubuque county from 6 AM through the day on February 16th. This snow continued to cause dangerous travel conditions through the day, into the evening commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection began in early afternoon in eastern Iowa along a subtle boundary across the state. This was fueled by high CAPE exceeding 3000 J/kg with deep layer shear sufficient to support large hail growth. Later in the evening a secondary round of convection moved across the area producing additional hail in the form of a cold pool driven MCS, driven along the plume of higher instability. The result was dozen of hail reports up to baseball sized across much of central and southern Iowa, damaging homes and vehicles and taking down power lines. As storms crossed Interstate 80 an accident resulted in significant delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Image received via twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave passed across the area, coincident with a low level plume of moisture. Moderate instability was in place with CAPE around 1500 J/kg but very weak flow and shear. While this meant that storms generally remained sub severe, the storms were also slow moving. One storm that set up over Fort Doge resulted in heavy rain with over three inches observed in one hour. Flooding occurred throughout the town with several road closures and a few stranded vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall of 3.33 inches so far, rain still in progress.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture transport phased with an elongated trough across the central United States. The surface low quickly deepened across the central plains, bringing a variety of precipitation impacts to Iowa. Precipitation began as rain in southern Iowa with a transition to freezing rain as temperatures fell. Across northern Iowa, precipitation fell primarily as snow with totals of four to nine inches reported. Towards east central Iowa, lack of ice introduction aloft resulted in period of heavy sleet and freezing rain. At the same time, winds through the day gusted 35 to 45 mph across the entire state, causing drifting snow and significantly reducing visibility at times. Many locations reported a quarter up to a half inch of ice accumulation. Dozens of car accidents occurred as a result and many schools were closed.

A trough lifted of of the four corners region and into the midwest, initially pulling a frontogenetical band of brief heavy snow across southern Iowa on the night of February 15th. Behind this, isentropic lift with deep saturation and lift through the dendritic growth zone allowed for heavy snow rates through the day on February 16th. By later afternoon into early evening snow began to taper off from west to east, leaving behind a wide swath of seven to nine inches of snow across southern Iowa, generally along and south of the interstate 80 corridor.

A large low pressure system lifted northeast into northern Illinois Thursday, February 9th, bringing a swath of snow and sleet of 5 to 9 inches from eastern Iowa into southwest Wisconsin. The combination of snow/sleet and strong winds produced hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Storm Spotter measured 3.6 inches of snow falling between 6 AM and Noon February 9th.  The combination of snow and strong winds was also reported by spotters and other COOP observers in the area, with generally 3 to 6 inches of snow and sleet reported.  The heavy wet snow created 100 percent snow and ice covered roads across Cedar County and western Clinton County according to the Iowa DOT.

The combination of snow and strong winds was reported by spotters and other COOP observers in the area, with generally 3 to 6 inch amounts.  The heavy wet snow created 100 percent snow and ice covered road conditions across Jones County, according to the Iowa DOT .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection began in early afternoon in eastern Iowa along a subtle boundary across the state. This was fueled by high CAPE exceeding 3000 J/kg with deep layer shear sufficient to support large hail growth. Later in the evening a secondary round of convection moved across the area producing additional hail in the form of a cold pool driven MCS, driven along the plume of higher instability. The result was dozen of hail reports up to baseball sized across much of central and southern Iowa, damaging homes and vehicles and taking down power lines. As storms crossed Interstate 80 an accident resulted in significant delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection percolated along a warm front across the area through the morning. By afternoon, storms re-strengthened as as daytime heating helped enhance instability. Despite good instability with MLCAPE over nearing 2000 J/kg, lapse rates were modest and speed shear was weak. Despite this, directional shear was good and when combined with instability for brief stretching, allowed for a number of weak and short-lived tornadoes to develop. While 13 tornadoes occurred, they were confined to two small areas in Iowa where storms trained, resulting in several storms over the same locations. In addition, the training storms made for pockets of heavy rain with three to four inches or more of rain. This did result in flash flooding in Pocahontas County with impacts to homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado occurred north of Highway 5 captured by many chasers. There was some tree damage produced by the tornado before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began southwest of Hills and moved northeast, crossing Highway 218 through the northwest portions of town. The tornado destroyed several outbuildings, large trees, and trim/siding on houses. The greatest damage was seen in western portions of the town of Hills, where roofs were completely torn off, siding removed from several homes and a large outbuilding destroyed. The tornado then proceeded through a wooded area with several trees snapped. It then continued northeast, destroying siding and roofs on several homes on the extreme southeast side of Iowa City. The tornado continued northeast and crossed Herbert Hoover Highway before lifting.  Johnson County EMA provided outstanding support to determine the final portion of this track.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the tornado path was identified during a post-event analysis of Sentinel satellite data. Near the end of the path, some damage to trees was found, resulting in the EF0 rating.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, Iowa DOT, and the public reported snowfall of 6 to 10 inches falling over Cedar county from 1 AM through the day on February 16th. This snow continued to cause dangerous travel conditions through the day, into the evening commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow, and some mixed precipitation, to much of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri on February 16th, 2023.||Snow began during the early morning across much of east central Iowa, with several locations (especially around Iowa City) seeing snow amounts around 4-6 inches around 6:00 AM. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm developed in the axis of greatest elevated instability around 1000 J/kg CAPE and dropped occasional large hail moving southeast across northwest Iowa.
An isolated storm developed in the axis of greatest elevated instability around 1000 J/kg CAPE and dropped occasional large hail moving southeast across northwest Iowa.

Convection began in early afternoon in eastern Iowa along a subtle boundary across the state. This was fueled by high CAPE exceeding 3000 J/kg with deep layer shear sufficient to support large hail growth. Later in the evening a secondary round of convection moved across the area producing additional hail in the form of a cold pool driven MCS, driven along the plume of higher instability. The result was dozen of hail reports up to baseball sized across much of central and southern Iowa, damaging homes and vehicles and taking down power lines. As storms crossed Interstate 80 an accident resulted in significant delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Image received via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture transport phased with an elongated trough across the central United States. The surface low quickly deepened across the central plains, bringing a variety of precipitation impacts to Iowa. Precipitation began as rain in southern Iowa with a transition to freezing rain as temperatures fell. Across northern Iowa, precipitation fell primarily as snow with totals of four to nine inches reported. Towards east central Iowa, lack of ice introduction aloft resulted in period of heavy sleet and freezing rain. At the same time, winds through the day gusted 35 to 45 mph across the entire state, causing drifting snow and significantly reducing visibility at times. Many locations reported a quarter up to a half inch of ice accumulation. Dozens of car accidents occurred as a result and many schools were closed.

This was fueled by high CAPE exceeding 3000 J/kg with deep layer shear sufficient to support large hail growth. Later in the evening a secondary round of convection moved across the area producing additional hail in the form of a cold pool driven MCS, driven along the plume of higher instability. The result was dozen of hail reports up to baseball sized across much of central and southern Iowa, damaging homes and vehicles and taking down power lines. As storms crossed Interstate 80 an accident resulted in significant delays.

A trough lifted of of the four corners region and into the midwest, initially pulling a frontogenetical band of brief heavy snow across southern Iowa on the night of February 15th. Behind this, isentropic lift with deep saturation and lift through the dendritic growth zone allowed for heavy snow rates through the day on February 16th. By later afternoon into early evening snow began to taper off from west to east, leaving behind a wide swath of seven to nine inches of snow across southern Iowa, generally along and south of the interstate 80 corridor.

Deep moisture transport phased with an elongated trough across the central United States. The surface low quickly deepened across the central plains, bringing a variety of precipitation impacts to Iowa. Precipitation began as rain in southern Iowa with a transition to freezing rain as temperatures fell. Across northern Iowa, precipitation fell primarily as snow with totals of four to nine inches reported. Towards east central Iowa, lack of ice introduction aloft resulted in period of heavy sleet and freezing rain. At the same time, winds through the day gusted 35 to 45 mph across the entire state, causing drifting snow and significantly reducing visibility at times. Many locations reported a quarter up to a half inch of ice accumulation. Dozens of car accidents occurred as a result and many schools were closed.

A well advertised storm system brough a mix of precipitation across all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and northeast Missouri on February 22nd. Precipitation began as rain during the early morning, but quickly changed to a mix of sleet and freezing rain as it progressed north over areas along and north of Interstate 80. This precipitation continued throughout the day before tapering off during the mid to late evening hours. ||Given the amount of moisture associated with this system, rain and ice amounts were high. Rainfall totals of around 1.00 - 1.50 were observed along and east of the Mississippi River. Ice amounts of a few hundredths up to three tenths of an inch were observed, with the latter mainly along and north of the Highway 30 corridor. This ice led to slippery travel conditions and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported ice accumulation of 0.20 to 0.25 of an inch, mainly on elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow, and some mixed precipitation, to much of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri on February 16th, 2023.||Snow began during the early morning across much of east central Iowa, with several locations (especially around Iowa City) seeing snow amounts around 4-6 inches around 6:00 AM. Light snow continued throughout the morning before the arrival of a stronger band of snow by early afternoon. This band impacted the Cedar Rapids / Iowa City metros during the early-mid afternoon before reaching the Mississippi River / Quad Cities metro by late afternoon and early evening, which led to treacherous travel conditions for the evening commute.||Snowfall totals ranged from around 2-6 inches in the Quad Cities / Dubuque metros, to around 7-12 inches in the Cedar Rapids / Iowa City metros. Lesser snow amounts were observed across southeast Iowa due to sleet mixing in with snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, Iowa DOT, and the public reported snowfall of 5 to 9 inches falling over Clinton county from 2 AM through the day on February 16th. This snow continued to cause dangerous travel conditions through the day, into the evening commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow to portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois late Friday night and early Saturday morning. Precipitation began as rain that quickly changed over to snow early Saturday.  Heavy wet snow fell east of a line from Fairfield, to Iowa City and Dubuque, and generally north of a line from Burlington, Iowa to Galesburg, Illinois.  The heaviest snowfall amounts between 8 and 12 inches fell from extreme eastern Iowa in Cedar, Scott, and Clinton counties, to Carroll, Jo Daviess, and Stephenson counties in Illinois.  This heavy snow caused numerous accidents and downed several tree branches due to the weight of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, Cocorahs, and numerous public reports showed 4 to 10 inches of heavy wet snow fell early in the morning March 25th. This heavy snow caused dangerous travel early in the day, and brought down numerous tree branches.  The most snow fell in eastern Cedar County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought light to moderate snow to much of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on March 9th, 2023. Precipitation began as a mix of rain and snow as it overspread the region during the morning hours. Snow was observed mainly north of Highway 34 to start, with more of a rain, snow, and sleet mix to the south. As the day progressed, precipitation began to change to all snow as temperatures dropped. This snow continued for most well into the early evening before tapering off by midnight. Snow totals were highest along and north of Interstate 80 with a range of around 4-6 inches. Pockets of higher amounts were observed in northwest Illinois in Stephenson County, with the highest total so far measured just west of Freeport at 6.5 inches. Further south, amounts were less with more rain and sleet mixing with the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observers, trained spotters, and the public reported snowfall of 4 to 7 inches over Cedar County from the morning into the evening of March 9. This snow contributed to travel issues through the day and into the nighttime hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.

Convection began in early afternoon in eastern Iowa along a subtle boundary across the state. This was fueled by high CAPE exceeding 3000 J/kg with deep layer shear sufficient to support large hail growth. Later in the evening a secondary round of convection moved across the area producing additional hail in the form of a cold pool driven MCS, driven along the plume of higher instability. The result was dozen of hail reports up to baseball sized across much of central and southern Iowa, damaging homes and vehicles and taking down power lines. As storms crossed Interstate 80 an accident resulted in significant delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported strong winds with potential damage and large hail with wide range of sizes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms developed in the exit region of a strong jet streak in northwest flow aloft, focused along a prefrontal trough as it moved from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa. Storms quickly became severe, with MLCAPE of up to 1500 J/kg and deep layer shear around 40 knots.

Later in the evening a secondary round of convection moved across the area producing additional hail in the form of a cold pool driven MCS, driven along the plume of higher instability. The result was dozen of hail reports up to baseball sized across much of central and southern Iowa, damaging homes and vehicles and taking down power lines. As storms crossed Interstate 80 an accident resulted in significant delays.

A trough lifted of of the four corners region and into the midwest, initially pulling a frontogenetical band of brief heavy snow across southern Iowa on the night of February 15th. Behind this, isentropic lift with deep saturation and lift through the dendritic growth zone allowed for heavy snow rates through the day on February 16th. By later afternoon into early evening snow began to taper off from west to east, leaving behind a wide swath of seven to nine inches of snow across southern Iowa, generally along and south of the interstate 80 corridor.

Deep moisture transport phased with an elongated trough across the central United States. The surface low quickly deepened across the central plains, bringing a variety of precipitation impacts to Iowa. Precipitation began as rain in southern Iowa with a transition to freezing rain as temperatures fell. Across northern Iowa, precipitation fell primarily as snow with totals of four to nine inches reported. Towards east central Iowa, lack of ice introduction aloft resulted in period of heavy sleet and freezing rain. At the same time, winds through the day gusted 35 to 45 mph across the entire state, causing drifting snow and significantly reducing visibility at times. Many locations reported a quarter up to a half inch of ice accumulation. Dozens of car accidents occurred as a result and many schools were closed.

A winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow, and some mixed precipitation, to much of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri on February 16th, 2023.||Snow began during the early morning across much of east central Iowa, with several locations (especially around Iowa City) seeing snow amounts around 4-6 inches around 6:00 AM. Light snow continued throughout the morning before the arrival of a stronger band of snow by early afternoon. This band impacted the Cedar Rapids / Iowa City metros during the early-mid afternoon before reaching the Mississippi River / Quad Cities metro by late afternoon and early evening, which led to treacherous travel conditions for the evening commute.||Snowfall totals ranged from around 2-6 inches in the Quad Cities / Dubuque metros, to around 7-12 inches in the Cedar Rapids / Iowa City metros. Lesser snow amounts were observed across southeast Iowa due to sleet mixing in with snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, Iowa DOT, and the public reported snowfall of 4 to 7.5 inches falling over Linn county from around 4 AM through the day on February 16th. This snow continued to cause dangerous travel conditions through the day, into the evening commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well advertised storm system brough a mix of precipitation across all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and northeast Missouri on February 22nd. Precipitation began as rain during the early morning, but quickly changed to a mix of sleet and freezing rain as it progressed north over areas along and north of Interstate 80. This precipitation continued throughout the day before tapering off during the mid to late evening hours. ||Given the amount of moisture associated with this system, rain and ice amounts were high. Rainfall totals of around 1.00 - 1.50 were observed along and east of the Mississippi River. Ice amounts of a few hundredths up to three tenths of an inch were observed, with the latter mainly along and north of the Highway 30 corridor. This ice led to slippery travel conditions and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported ice accumulation of 0.20 to 0.25 of an inch, mainly on elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained early in the month but persistent precipitation and some thaw allowed improvement during much of February.  Conditions improved with severe drought ending in these areas by February 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wright County started in severe drought to begin February but wet conditions persisted through the middle of the month along with some thaw.  This allowed infiltration of water into the ground and improved drought conditions by the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought moderate to heavy snow to portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois late Friday night and early Saturday morning. Precipitation began as rain that quickly changed over to snow early Saturday.  Heavy wet snow fell east of a line from Fairfield, to Iowa City and Dubuque, and generally north of a line from Burlington, Iowa to Galesburg, Illinois.  The heaviest snowfall amounts between 8 and 12 inches fell from extreme eastern Iowa in Cedar, Scott, and Clinton counties, to Carroll, Jo Daviess, and Stephenson counties in Illinois.  This heavy snow caused numerous accidents and downed several tree branches due to the weight of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, Cocorahs, and numerous public reports showed 7 to 11.5 inches of heavy wet snow fell early in the morning March 25th. This heavy snow caused dangerous travel early in the day, and brought down numerous tree branches.  The most snow fell in northern Scott County.

A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A review of Sentinel satellite data confirms that a separate tornado developed near Amish, and moved northeast around 6 miles to near Frytown, before dissipating. The tornado caused damage to farm outbuildings along its path. The tornado was rated EF0 with maximum winds around 85 mph. After the tornado dissipated, the associated supercell thunderstorm cycled again and produced an EF2 tornado that tracked from Coralville to Solon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions averaged cooler than normal (2 to 3 degrees below normal) and dry (50-75 percent of normal) across northwest Iowa during July and resulted in generally unchanged drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through July.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) conditions during late June.

This band impacted the Cedar Rapids / Iowa City metros during the early-mid afternoon before reaching the Mississippi River / Quad Cities metro by late afternoon and early evening, which led to treacherous travel conditions for the evening commute.||Snowfall totals ranged from around 2-6 inches in the Quad Cities / Dubuque metros, to around 7-12 inches in the Cedar Rapids / Iowa City metros. Lesser snow amounts were observed across southeast Iowa due to sleet mixing in with snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, Iowa DOT, and the public reported snowfall of 6 to 8.5 inches falling over Iowa county from around 1 AM through the day on February 16th. This snow continued to cause dangerous travel conditions through the day, into the evening commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection began in early afternoon in eastern Iowa along a subtle boundary across the state. This was fueled by high CAPE exceeding 3000 J/kg with deep layer shear sufficient to support large hail growth. Later in the evening a secondary round of convection moved across the area producing additional hail in the form of a cold pool driven MCS, driven along the plume of higher instability. The result was dozen of hail reports up to baseball sized across much of central and southern Iowa, damaging homes and vehicles and taking down power lines. As storms crossed Interstate 80 an accident resulted in significant delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain and anomalously warm temperatures melting a deeper than average snowpack during April resulted in third to fifth highest crests on record and moderate to major flooding at sites along the Upper Mississippi River in Iowa. Initial minor flood stage was reached in the middle of April with near record crests towards the end of April. In response to the flooding a Proclamation of Disaster Emergency was issued for counties along the Mississippi River by the Governor of Iowa. Drier conditions for the latter half of April and well below normal rainfall through May allowed the Upper Mississippi River to steadily recede, dropping below minor flood stage at sites along the Mississippi River in Iowa in the middle of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the third highest crest on record at the McGregor Dam towards the end of April. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage at the McGregor Dam on May 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low moved from Colorado into south-central Nebraska and occluded. A very narrow snowband (approximately 30 to 40 miles wide) developed north of the occlusion. The peak reported amount was 7 inches in Harrison County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer reported 7-inches of snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 3-inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system tracked across the state of Iowa on Friday, March 31st. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist conditions into the Midwest, which coupled with a very favorable wind profile and created quite the volatile environment for severe thunderstorms. This produced several long lived supercells which developed across central Iowa during the afternoon.  The storms were prolific hail producers along with a few reports of wind damage as they moved rapidly northeast.  Eventually, the storms produced a few tornadoes before exiting into eastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clarke County EMA reporting from conservation land on the Lucas Clarke county line on highway 34. Time estimated by radar.

Severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought conditions at the start of November remained constant across northeast Iowa through November from long-standing abnormally dry conditions. While the previous month of October provided near to slightly above normal rainfall amounts, total rainfall near 20 inches from April 1 to October 31st was 9 to 12 inches below normal. During November, only 1 to 3 days of measurable precipitation provided 0.1 to 0.5 inches total rainfall, near 2 inches below the monthly normal. While the growing season was all but over, multiple days of above normal temperatures, 10 to 20 degrees above normal, inhibited the ground from freezing. The drought remained unchanged through November as county-encompassing extreme drought (D3) extended from Mitchell and Floyd counties eastward to Winneshiek and Fayette counties. Widespread severe drought (D2) continued in Allamakee and Clayton counties with areas of extreme drought (D3).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought persisted across all of Allamakee County through November. The combination of total monthly precipitation under 0.2 inches, more than 2 inches below normal in spots, and several days of anomalously warm temperatures resulted in 20 to 30% below normal topsoil moisture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long track tornado began in Wapello County northeast |of Ottumwa then tracked northeast into Keokuk County. |The primary damage in Wapello County consisted of three |homes and one hog confinement facility. The first home |lost the entire roof and one external wall. The first failure |point was likely the wind entering and lifting the garage |which then led to failure of the roof. The home only had |straight nailed connections to the external walls spaced|every 18 inches and less to the roof. An unoccupied |mobile home on the property was not well anchored and |rolled to total destruction. This location was rated 110 mph |and EF-1. Two well anchored hog confinement buildings |were totally destroyed and were rated EF-2. The tornado |then impacted a location with a well built 2 story brick |house. The house suffered some roof and window damage |and a loss of a garage door but sustained little other |damage. Other damage indicators at the site suggested |EF-1 winds. Total destruction of a house occurred just |prior to the tornado exiting the county. This older house |sat unanchored to the concrete block basement. The house |was pushed off the foundation and crumbled. Some trees |near the house and a propane tank were mostly left with |little damage. A larger tree directly next to the house on |the wind side had several large limbs removed. Due to |all of these reasons, the wind at this location has been |rated at 135 mph, or high end EF-2.

A quick moving shortwave lifted across the central plains and into Iowa on the morning of March 26th. Strong frontogenesis on the leading edge allowed for a banded snow scenario with strong lift within the dendritic growth zone. This brought a narrow band, generally 35 miles wide, of snow across central Iowa with five to nine inches of snow from Crawford to Black Hawk County.

The combination of heavy rain and anomalously warm temperatures melting a deeper than average snowpack during April resulted in third to fifth highest crests on record and moderate to major flooding at sites along the Upper Mississippi River in Iowa. Initial minor flood stage was reached in the middle of April with near record crests towards the end of April. In response to the flooding a Proclamation of Disaster Emergency was issued for counties along the Mississippi River by the Governor of Iowa. Drier conditions for the latter half of April and well below normal rainfall through May allowed the Upper Mississippi River to steadily recede, dropping below minor flood stage at sites along the Mississippi River in Iowa in the middle of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the fourth highest crest on record in Lansing towards the end of April. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage in Lansing on May 4th.

Drier conditions for the latter half of April and well below normal rainfall through May allowed the Upper Mississippi River to steadily recede, dropping below minor flood stage at sites along the Mississippi River in Iowa in the middle of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the third highest crest on record at the Guttenberg Dam towards the end of April. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage at the Guttenberg Dam on May 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system brought severe weather to parts of northeast Iowa during the late afternoon of Friday, March 31, 2023. Reports of 1.0 to 1.25 inch hail were reported in Mitchell and Floyd Counties. The largest hail reported was half dollar in Stacyville (Mitchell County) and Colwell (Floyd County). An EF0 tornado entered rural Clayton County out of Delaware County near Edgewood and dissipated shortly thereafter, resulting in minimal damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported near Colwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four consecutive days of light to heavy rainfall events during the middle of May resulted in near 1.5 inches and some minor flooding along the more narrow, shallow rivers in northeast Iowa. Initial light to moderate rainfall on May 12th quenched soils from a drier than normal spring, while subsequent storms over the weekend of May 13th-14th resulted in rapid runoff and minor flooding along the Little Cedar and Turkey Rivers. Along the Little Cedar River, the highest realized height above flood stage occurred in Nashua (Chickasaw County) on May 16th with 0.94 feet. Along the Turkey River, the river gauge in Cresco (Howard County) crested 0.57 feet above flood stage on May 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant runoff from heavy rainfall upstream resulted in a rapid rise of water and minor flooding along the Little Cedar River near Orchard on May 15th. Minor flooding was initially realized late on May 14th, with the river cresting 0.51 feet above flood stage of 1081.5 feet above sea level during the early morning of May 15th. It then fell below flood stage later that morning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers in Howard County reported 0.97 inches of rainfall near Cresco on May 12th as well as minor flooding in low lying areas near Davis Corners on May 14th. As a result of the heavy rain, the Turkey River in Cresco rapidly rose on May 14th, cresting 0.57 feet above minor flood stage of 1128.5 feet above sea level late that night. It then fell below flood stage during the early morning of May 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another large, violent tornado developed in eastern Keokuk County as the initial tornado was weakening southwest of Keota. The tornado tracked to the northeast, on the western fringe of Keota, and crossed into Washington County near the intersection of Keokuk-Washington Road and 180th St. The tornado then continued through northwest Washington county, extreme southeast Iowa County, and into far southwest Johnson County. The tornado severely damaged several homes near Keota, wiping one house completely off its foundation, resulting in EF-4 level damage. A car was lofted in the air and tossed about 1000 feet into a nearby field and trees were completely debarked with only stubs of the largest branches remaining. As the tornado tracked west of Wellman, Iowa it knocked over a 325 ft cell phone tower. The tornado began to dissipate as it tracked into far southwest Johnson County. The tornado was rated EF-4 in Keokuk County, EF-3 in Washington County, and EF-1 in Iowa and Johnson Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unorganized group of storms merged and surged across far eastern Nebraska and into western Iowa and produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management relayed photos of numerous downed trees and branches in Thurman.
