EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed just southwest of Coralville, crossed Highway 218 and damaged the James St. Warehouse near Highway 6 in Coralville. Several trailers and a truck were flipped over in the vicinity. A retail building also had part of a roof lifted off with several vehicles rolled or flipped. The tornado then tracked into a residential area and uprooted and snapped numerous trees. At least 2 injuries were reported by emergency management. Damage then became more sporadic before the tornado reintensified across Coralville Reservoir. A more concentrated path began near Sugar Bottom campground east of North Liberty and continued northeast towards Solon. A radio tower collapsed and a roof was lifted off a home and partially removed. The tornado continued northeast into a field before eventually reaching another farmstead and destroying an outbuilding. Debris from the farmstead was lofted into a field with a 2x4 driven into the ground. The tornado continued into Solon, uprooting trees, bending street signs, and destroying another outbuilding before dissipating and lifting just north of Sutliff Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms initiated west of the area over portions of Nebraska and Kansas in the overnight hours of the 28th into the 29th, eventually leading into and across southern Iowa during the morning hours of the 29th. These storms were situated within a fairly supportive environment, including a north south gradient of MUCAPE values in the 2000 to 4000+ J/kg range, 30 to 40+ kts of effective shear, and steering flow supportive of MCS maintenance. Eventually, linear storm mode evolved into an MCV. Resulting storms produced a number of strong wind and wind damage reports, including trees downed and measured winds to 67 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lamoni Municipal Airport ASOS recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt from a few day period of well above normal temperatures focused on the 11-13th of April resulted in minor flooding for portions of the Rock River Basin. Some areas north of I-90 lost between 2 to 5 inches of snow water equivalent (SWE) from the first to second week of April, but antecedent drought conditions resulted in only a percentage of this water actually making it into the river system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River near Rock Rapids, IA rose above flood stage on the evening of April 10th before cresting around 0.4 feet above flood stage (13.4 feet) on April 13th. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of April 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms initiated west of the area over portions of Nebraska and Kansas in the overnight hours of the 28th into the 29th, eventually leading into and across southern Iowa during the morning hours of the 29th. These storms were situated within a fairly supportive environment, including a north south gradient of MUCAPE values in the 2000 to 4000+ J/kg range, 30 to 40+ kts of effective shear, and steering flow supportive of MCS maintenance. Eventually, linear storm mode evolved into an MCV. Resulting storms produced a number of strong wind and wind damage reports, including trees downed and measured winds to 67 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported power lines and trees down. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening upper level low pressure area moved into far southeast South Dakota by early evening. Slow moving thunderstorms developed within a very narrow axis which nosed into Buena Vista County, where the environment featured maximized and collocated 0-3km shear and 0-3km CAPE, and resulted in a few brief tornadoes connected with storm development phase.  In addition, very efficient thermodynamic profiles resulted in extremely heavy rainfall rates, with observed rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up just north of the Sac-Buena Vista County border between 90th and 100th Avenue. The circulation featured intermittent contact with the surface for the next mile, as the tornado tracked a bit more westward after crossing 90th Avenue. There were no damage indicators impacted along the track, which was documented fully on storm chaser video. An EF rating could not be determined and the average path width was estimated to be 50 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado quickly spun up east of 230th Avenue and north of 620th Street in an open field. The tornado traveled to the north-northwest for several minutes, then crossed roadways near the intersection of 230th Avenue and 600th Street.  The tornado impacted a small farmstead on the west side of 230th Avenue where it tore several shingles off an old barn and damaged numerous branches and several rotted trees. The tornado continued another quarter mile to the northwest before lifting. The maximum wind speed was 75 mph and the average path width was 50 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just south of Bellevue, and moved northeast over the Mississippi River before dissipating. This tornado impacted an RV park and some cabins located nearby. Some structural damage was noted, with some RVs that were flipped and damaged. Several trees within the area were also impacted, with some uprooted. Three  injuries were noted with this tornado in the vicinity of the RV park. This tornado continued into Jo Daviess County, Illinois, but likely dissipated over the river as no damage was observed in a wooded area adjacent to the river. Peak winds were estimated around 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 3 miles north of Atalissa, Iowa in Cedar County, snapping power poles and large trees along its path. It caused significant damage to two homes southwest of Bennett, Iowa, and flipped over a semi along interstate 80. It also damaged several concrete block silos south of Bennett. The tornado continued to track to the northeast through a rural area, causing sporadic tree damage.  The tornado entered extreme northwest Scott County about a third of a mile south of 220th St., then travelled around one mile before entering Clinton County about a half mile west of the intersection of 280th St. and 110th Ave. The tornado then dissipated about 2 miles south of Wheatland. The peak winds were estimated at 120 mph. The tornado was rated EF-2 in Cedar County, and EF-0 in Scott and Clinton Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In semi similar fashion to the strong and severe storms experienced on the previous morning (29th of June), storms initiated over portions of northern Kansas and southern Nebraska in conjunction with the low level jet in the very early hours of the 29th. These storms, steered by easterly mean flow, progressed across far southern Iowa and northern Missouri within an environment consisting of around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE, 30 to 40+ kts of effective shear, and a very tight north-south gradient of DCAPE (most favorably into Missouri). Storms grew upscale through the early morning hours and primarily produced strong and damaging winds. There were a couple of severe hail reports as well along with a reported lightning strike that effective blew a tree apart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported tree branch down on powerlines. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) moved out of Nebraska and South Dakota, into Iowa in the afternoon of June 17th. Increased forcing with the MCV met with increasing instability as CAPE reach 1500-2000 J/kg in the afternoon. Storms developed quickly in a line, with a few isolated cells remaining on the southern end of the line. While deep shear was only around 25 kts, this was enough to sustain updraft capable of producing severe hail. Several locations in southwest Iowa reported quarter sized hail with one instance of thunderstorm wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large upper level ridging was in place for several days in late May to early June. With little movement in the Great Lakes area surface high, Iowa experiences afternoon thunderstorms largely driven by good moisture transport and diurnal heating each day. On the afternoon of June 2nd, a couple storms manages to strengthen more than the others scattered across the area, producing damaging wind gusts in Des Moines and Marshalltown, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of a large, healthy tree snapped at the trunk near Ferguson. Estimated sized was 2.5 feet in diameter. Report via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The surface pattern continued to be dominated by high pressure to the east over the Great Lakes region. As was typical in the preceding days, afternoon thunderstorms developed as diurnal heating peaked. On the 4th, however, storms were also aided by a weak boundary moving across the area. Storms developing along this boundary remained below severe criteria, but one did manage to produce nickel sized hail in Ames, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail and winds estimated near 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In semi similar fashion to the strong and severe storms experienced on the previous morning (29th of June), storms initiated over portions of northern Kansas and southern Nebraska in conjunction with the low level jet in the very early hours of the 29th. These storms, steered by easterly mean flow, progressed across far southern Iowa and northern Missouri within an environment consisting of around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE, 30 to 40+ kts of effective shear, and a very tight north-south gradient of DCAPE (most favorably into Missouri). Storms grew upscale through the early morning hours and primarily produced strong and damaging winds. There were a couple of severe hail reports as well along with a reported lightning strike that effective blew a tree apart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public relayed photo of a tree blown apart by a lightning strike. No other apparent damage to the residence at the time. Time matched to CG lightning data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening. After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole along 80th Avenue just south of Iowa Highway 3 was leaning, nearby corn was flattened, and large tree branches were broken due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt from a few day period of well above normal temperatures focused on the 11-13th of April resulted in minor to locally moderate flooding through much of the Big Sioux River Basin. Some areas north of I-90 lost between 2 to 5 inches of snow water equivalent (SWE) from the first to second week of April, but antecedent drought conditions resulted in only a percentage of this water actually making it into the river system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Canton, SD rose above flood stage on the morning of April 10th before cresting around 1.02 feet above flood stage (1236.02 feet) on April 10th. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of April 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last of the seasonal snow cover melted off, and even with precipitation only at 75 percent or less of normal, near average temperatures kept drought conditions stable from last month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions remained in place through April 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level wave moving through southwest flow aloft, acting on an environment with 1000 to 1500 J/kg of MUCAPE in the vicinity of a surface trough, resulted in scattered thunderstorms across far southeast South Dakota, far northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota during the evening of April 19th. The strongest storms produced severe hail ranging from 1.0 to 1.5 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail 1.0 inch in diameter fell 7 miles southeast of Westfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gradient around a large upper level circulation resulted in widespread winds of 30 to 40 mph and gusts from 45 to 65 mph east of the James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted as high as 45 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site KCKP in Cherokee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt during late March into early April, as well as some added rainfall, resulted in rises on some small streams and creeks across the region. Some of these rises pushed toward or into minor flood stage for a short period, but overall impacts were limited mainly to agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River near Milford, IA rose above flood stage on the evening of April 1st before cresting around 1.59 feet above flood stage (13.59 feet) on April 5th. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of April 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms tracked across southeast Iowa during the mid-afternoon into early evening of June 24, producing large hail up to golf ball size in Van Buren County and tennis ball size hail near Ottumwa.||More organized clusters of severe storms tracked through Dubuque into far northwest IL during the evening and late night hours of June 24.  Another storm cluster moved through the Quad Cities, producing wind gusts to 70 mph at the Davenport ASOS and damage to trees and power poles.  An EF-0 tornado also tracked east along and north of I-80 near the Davenport Airport, causing sporadic minor tree and structure damage.  This storm impacted the Quad City Airshow grounds, while no visitors were present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inch diameter tree branches blown down, along with lots of smaller branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, June 29, a powerful severe wind event moved over the region. This event was known as a derecho given its long duration from north of Kansas City to southern Indiana and its consistent strong intensity. Almost all of the damage in the area surveyed locally was due to straight-line winds, at times easily peaking between 100-120 mph in far western Illinois. Those who experienced the event conveyed how the strongest winds persisted for 10 to 15 minutes, and in some locations, it lasted as long as 20 minutes, which is atypical for derecho events.||There were some swaths of enhanced damage. These were largely due to intense wind surges in the local area. One of these in Clark county, Mo was determined to be a short-lived EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power out, with 3 trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions averaged cooler than normal (2 to 3 degrees below normal) and dry (50-75 percent of normal) across northwest Iowa during July and resulted in generally unchanged drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system slowly dropping SE form the eastern Dakotas and SW Minnesota dragged a cold front across the state during the evening and overnight hours of the 24th of June. Within the warm sector, MUCAPE values peaked in excess of 3000 J/kg during peak heating and scaled back a bit overnight but remained around or in excess of 2000 J/kg. Effective shear values were supportive for organized convection, residing in the 35 to around 50 kt range. Morning and lingering convective activity likely played a role in initiating some storms with remnant outflow boundaries around. As a result, a number of isolated or clustered organized storms were seen during the evening and overnight hours and produced a number of reports of large hail and damaging winds. The largest hail reported was 3 inches in both Palo Alto and Black Hawk counties. A couple of funnel clouds were reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 1 inch hail, relayed from NWS Chat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were two rounds of severe thunderstorms on April 4. Round 1 of activity began during the early morning hours of April 4, 2023, as elevated thunderstorms developed across southeast Iowa. These storms quickly intensified and became severe as they moved into eastern Iowa, and produced very large hail from golf ball to tea cup size (2 inches). The largest hailstones were measured in and around the Davenport metro, where tea cup size hail was measured at the North Park Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms tracked across southeast Iowa during the mid-afternoon into early evening of June 24, producing large hail up to golf ball size in Van Buren County and tennis ball size hail near Ottumwa.||More organized clusters of severe storms tracked through Dubuque into far northwest IL during the evening and late night hours of June 24.  Another storm cluster moved through the Quad Cities, producing wind gusts to 70 mph at the Davenport ASOS and damage to trees and power poles.  An EF-0 tornado also tracked east along and north of I-80 near the Davenport Airport, causing sporadic minor tree and structure damage.  This storm impacted the Quad City Airshow grounds, while no visitors were present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed about 2 miles east of Walcott along Interstate 80. The tornado tracked east and caused sporadic minor tree and structure damage at the Northwest Boulevard exit just north of I-80. The tornado then moved across the Davenport Airport, and caused damage to canopies, tents, and equipment, which was hosting an airshow. The tornado passed just south of the NWS office, then continued east, producing minor tree and structure damage along 210th St. The tornado followed an intermittent path of around 9.6 miles, and was on the ground intermittently for about 14 minutes. The tornado was rated EF-0, with maximum estimated winds around 75 mph. Occasional minor tree damage was also observed south of the path, due to straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, June 29, a powerful severe wind event moved over the region. This event was known as a derecho given its long duration from north of Kansas City to southern Indiana and its consistent strong intensity. Almost all of the damage in the area surveyed locally was due to straight-line winds, at times easily peaking between 100-120 mph in far western Illinois. Those who experienced the event conveyed how the strongest winds persisted for 10 to 15 minutes, and in some locations, it lasted as long as 20 minutes, which is atypical for derecho events.||There were some swaths of enhanced damage. These were largely due to intense wind surges in the local area. One of these in Clark county, Mo was determined to be a short-lived EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down, onto a power line.

There were two rounds of severe thunderstorms on April 4. Round 1 of activity began during the early morning hours of April 4, 2023, as elevated thunderstorms developed across southeast Iowa. These storms quickly intensified and became severe as they moved into eastern Iowa, and produced very large hail from golf ball to tea cup size (2 inches). The largest hailstones were measured in and around the Davenport metro, where tea cup size hail was measured at the North Park Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak impulse dropped southward along the western side of a Great Lakes closed mid-level low, igniting isolated storms overnight. Weak shear and evaporation within a low-level dry airmass resulted in localized damaging wind gusts with the strongest storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees and a grain bin were damaged by thunderstorm winds. Property and crop damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of showers and thunderstorms from May 11-14th resulted in locally heavy rainfall that pushed portions of the Little Sioux River into minor flood stage. Flooding was largely limited to agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River at Spencer, IA rose above flood stage on the morning of May 14 before cresting around 0.89 feet above flood stage (10.89 feet) on May 16th. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of May 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred on the mainstem Mississippi largely due to the record snowfall this winter in Minnesota and Wisconsin, frozen ground, rapid snowmelt during the middle of April, and saturated soils.  Major flooding was observed at all sites along the Mississippi north of Keokuk Iowa and most sites saw one of their top 10 crests on record.|This flooding had numerous impacts, with many roads, businesses, homes, and people affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at New Boston rose above its major flood stage of 18.5 feet during the early morning hours of April 26th,  The river continued to rise to 20.6 feet late in the day on April 30th.|The Mississippi River at Keithsburg rose above its major flood stage of 17.0 feet during the early morning hours of April 26th and continued to rise to 18.88 feet by the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Rock Island Lock and Dam 15 rose above its major flood stage of 18.0 feet around mid day on  April 24th and continued to rise to its crest of 21.41 on the evening of April 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, June 29, a powerful severe wind event moved over the region. This event was known as a derecho given its long duration from north of Kansas City to southern Indiana and its consistent strong intensity. Almost all of the damage in the area surveyed locally was due to straight-line winds, at times easily peaking between 100-120 mph in far western Illinois. Those who experienced the event conveyed how the strongest winds persisted for 10 to 15 minutes, and in some locations, it lasted as long as 20 minutes, which is atypical for derecho events.||There were some swaths of enhanced damage. These were largely due to intense wind surges in the local area. One of these in Clark county, Mo was determined to be a short-lived EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey found damage to dozens of large trees and utility poles consistent|with peak wind gusts of 90 to 100 mph throughout the town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of showers and thunderstorms from May 11-14th resulted in locally heavy rainfall that pushed portions of the Little Sioux River into minor flood stage. Flooding was largely limited to agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River above Spencer, IA rose above flood stage on the morning of May 14 before cresting around 0.59 feet above flood stage (1322.59 feet) on May 15th. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of May 18th.

Another storm cluster moved through the Quad Cities, producing wind gusts to 70 mph at the Davenport ASOS and damage to trees and power poles.  An EF-0 tornado also tracked east along and north of I-80 near the Davenport Airport, causing sporadic minor tree and structure damage.  This storm impacted the Quad City Airshow grounds, while no visitors were present.

The largest hailstones were measured in and around the Davenport metro, where tea cup size hail was measured at the North Park Mall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report was relayed by an off duty NWS Employee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system slowly dropping SE form the eastern Dakotas and SW Minnesota dragged a cold front across the state during the evening and overnight hours of the 24th of June. Within the warm sector, MUCAPE values peaked in excess of 3000 J/kg during peak heating and scaled back a bit overnight but remained around or in excess of 2000 J/kg. Effective shear values were supportive for organized convection, residing in the 35 to around 50 kt range. Morning and lingering convective activity likely played a role in initiating some storms with remnant outflow boundaries around. As a result, a number of isolated or clustered organized storms were seen during the evening and overnight hours and produced a number of reports of large hail and damaging winds. The largest hail reported was 3 inches in both Palo Alto and Black Hawk counties. A couple of funnel clouds were reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported golf ball sized hail via mPing app.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In semi similar fashion to the strong and severe storms experienced on the previous morning (29th of June), storms initiated over portions of northern Kansas and southern Nebraska in conjunction with the low level jet in the very early hours of the 29th. These storms, steered by easterly mean flow, progressed across far southern Iowa and northern Missouri within an environment consisting of around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE, 30 to 40+ kts of effective shear, and a very tight north-south gradient of DCAPE (most favorably into Missouri). Storms grew upscale through the early morning hours and primarily produced strong and damaging winds. There were a couple of severe hail reports as well along with a reported lightning strike that effective blew a tree apart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail. Also estimated wind gusts to around 40 mph at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level jet within the presence of a sharp lower- to mid-level boundary focused widespread convective development during the overnight hours. Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear in excess of 40 kts produced a very organized bowing system, which resulted in widespread 60 to 80 mph wind reports in southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa. At the peak, several thousand people were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts as high as 59 mph were measured by Automated Surface Observing System site KSUX 2 miles southwest of Sergeant Bluff.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite a few different periods of rain during the month, severe drought conditions persisted in Pocahontas County during July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid air in place across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri interacted with a southward moving frontal boundary and disturbance aloft, setting the stage for development and organization of severe thunderstorms across the region. Storms initially formed from north central Iowa and southern Wisconsin along the frontal boundary, which slowly worked south into the hot, humid air in place across the region. As storms arrived across east central Iowa, they began producing winds around 70 MPH across the Hwy 20 corridor. Another storm to the south further intensified, and began producing winds of 80+ MPH as it tracked along the Hwy 30 corridor. This impacted the northern suburbs of Cedar Rapids into Clinton. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level jet within the presence of a sharp lower- to mid-level boundary focused widespread convective development during the overnight hours. Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear in excess of 40 kts produced a very organized bowing system, which resulted in widespread 60 to 80 mph wind reports in southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa. At the peak, several thousand people were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts as high as 66 mph were measured by Iowa Road Weather Information System site RSLI4 2 miles northeast of Truesdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and elevated near surface moisture resulted in a multi-day heat wave across southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska from July 25-28. With air temperatures rising into the 90s and low 100s and dewpoint temperatures in the 70s and low 80s, afternoon and evening heat indices generally peaked from 100 to 120 degrees with little overnight recovery. The most widespread opressive conditions occurred during the afternoons and evenings of July 26 and July 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 120 degrees with a maximum heat index of 120 degrees at the Iowa Department of Transportation site near Moville on July 27 and July 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak warm advection continued to push across Iowa beneath thermal ridging, especially towards northwest Iowa into Minnesota. By evening convection had developed along this gradient. While much of this remained sub-severe and confined to Minnesota, a few isolated storms did develop in northwest Iowa with one resulting in quarter sized hail.

A cold front across South Dakota and Minnesota dropped south across Iowa on the evening of July 10th. Environmental parameters were marginal with CAPE around 1000 J/kg and deep layer shear around 20 to 25 knots. Convergence along the front allowed for storms to develop in the late afternoon, moving across northern and northwest Iowa. Storms strengthened to severe criteria across Emmet county, where hail up to baseball sized was reported along with a wind gust of 58 mph at the Estherville ASOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report, time estimated by radar.

Another storm to the south further intensified, and began producing winds of 80+ MPH as it tracked along the Hwy 30 corridor. This impacted the northern suburbs of Cedar Rapids into Clinton. These storms continued to produce wind gusts around 60-70 MPH as they progressed into northwest Illinois. One brief EF-0 tornado began in Clinton, Iowa, then moved across the Mississippi River before dissipating near Fulton, Illinois. Damage near Andrew, Iowa in Jackson County was determined to be straight-line winds. Storms additionally produced large hail around the Dubuque metro, with quarter size hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down along Highway 62, near Andrew.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms developed in the exit region of a strong jet streak in northwest flow aloft, focused along a prefrontal trough as it moved from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa. Storms quickly became severe, with MLCAPE of up to 1500 J/kg and deep layer shear around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was forced to lean over and several large tree branches were broken by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm along a frontal boundary produced a local burst of damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 3 to 4 inch diameter tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front across South Dakota and Minnesota dropped south across Iowa on the evening of July 10th. Environmental parameters were marginal with CAPE around 1000 J/kg and deep layer shear around 20 to 25 knots. Convergence along the front allowed for storms to develop in the late afternoon, moving across northern and northwest Iowa. Storms strengthened to severe criteria across Emmet county, where hail up to baseball sized was reported along with a wind gust of 58 mph at the Estherville ASOS.

Strong high pressure and elevated near surface moisture resulted in a multi-day heat wave across southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska from July 25-28. With air temperatures rising into the 90s and low 100s and dewpoint temperatures in the 70s and low 80s, afternoon and evening heat indices generally peaked from 100 to 120 degrees with little overnight recovery. The most widespread opressive conditions occurred during the afternoons and evenings of July 26 and July 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 109 degrees with a maximum heat index of 109 degrees at the Iowa Department of Transportation site near Sibley on July 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms focused along a weak surface boundary from northwest Iowa into areas south of Interstate 90 in southeast South Dakota. Even with very weak deep-layer shear, modest surface-based instability of 1500-2000 J/kg led to the rapid development of storms, which peaked in mainly hail producing severity early in the life cycle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mainly pea-sized, with several stones as large as nickels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the latter half of the afternoon along a slow moving cold front.  Dry air poised off the surface allowed for outflow dominant storms to develop, with a brief period of damaging gusty wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several larger limbs were broken off trees by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave brushed across northern Iowa through the early morning hours of July 26th. Despite warm mid levels, storms were fueled by the low level jet and moved along a moderate instability gradient of 1500 J/kg of CAPE.  While most storms remained sub-severe, one storm across Pocahontas county strengthened to produce quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level jet within the presence of a sharp lower- to mid-level boundary focused widespread convective development during the overnight hours. Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear in excess of 40 kts produced a very organized bowing system, which resulted in widespread 60 to 80 mph wind reports in southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa. At the peak, several thousand people were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds were measured on a personal weather station in 4 miles north-northeast of Sergeant Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid air in place across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri interacted with a southward moving frontal boundary and disturbance aloft, setting the stage for development and organization of severe thunderstorms across the region. Storms initially formed from north central Iowa and southern Wisconsin along the frontal boundary, which slowly worked south into the hot, humid air in place across the region. As storms arrived across east central Iowa, they began producing winds around 70 MPH across the Hwy 20 corridor. Another storm to the south further intensified, and began producing winds of 80+ MPH as it tracked along the Hwy 30 corridor. This impacted the northern suburbs of Cedar Rapids into Clinton. These storms continued to produce wind gusts around 60-70 MPH as they progressed into northwest Illinois. One brief EF-0 tornado began in Clinton, Iowa, then moved across the Mississippi River before dissipating near Fulton, Illinois. Damage near Andrew, Iowa in Jackson County was determined to be straight-line winds. Storms additionally produced large hail around the Dubuque metro, with quarter size hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs down and power is out, due to 80 mph wind gusts.

Another in a series of jet streaks in northwest flow aloft initiated afternoon and early evening convection across eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Several storms in an environment of 1500-2000 MLCAPE and around 40 knots of distributed deep-layer shear took on supercellular characteristics and produced hail up to baseball size in southwest Minnesota.

A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado is a continuation of a tornado that began in Johnson County, just northwest of Solon. The tornado crossed into Linn County about a quarter mile north of the intersection of 110th St. NE and Taft Ave. NE. In Linn County, the tornado continued northeast and produced some tree, roof, and outbuilding damage before dissipating in the southern part of Mt. Vernon. The tornado was rated EF1 in Linn County, with maximum winds around 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level short-wave helped lead to clusters of weak thunderstorms the morning of August 2. A moisture rich environment led to efficient downpours from thunderstorms. Additionally, clusters tracked over the same locations helping lead to high rainfall totals. There were several reports of 3 to 4 inches of rainfall which led to flash flooding across the area. The highest report was an estimated 7-inches of rainfall near Walnut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report from the public of nearly 7-inches of rain just south of Walnut and that it was still raining heavily at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of showers and thunderstorms from May 11-14th resulted in locally heavy rainfall that pushed portions of the Little Sioux River into minor flood stage. Flooding was largely limited to agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River near Milford, IA rose above flood stage on the morning of May 14 before cresting around 1.49 feet above flood stage (13.49 feet) on May 16th. The river then fell below flood stage on the afternoon of May 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level short-wave helped lead to clusters of weak thunderstorms the morning of August 2. A moisture rich environment led to efficient downpours from thunderstorms. Additionally, clusters tracked over the same locations helping lead to high rainfall totals. There were several reports of 3 to 4 inches of rainfall which led to flash flooding across the area. The highest report was an estimated 7-inches of rainfall near Walnut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 4.38-inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another large, violent tornado developed in eastern Keokuk County as the initial tornado was weakening southwest of Keota. The tornado tracked to the northeast, on the western fringe of Keota, and crossed into Washington County near the intersection of Keokuk-Washington Road and 180th St. The tornado then continued through northwest Washington county, extreme southeast Iowa County, and into far southwest Johnson County. The tornado severely damaged several homes near Keota, wiping one house completely off its foundation, resulting in EF-4 level damage. A car was lofted in the air and tossed about 1000 feet into a nearby field and trees were completely debarked with only stubs of the largest branches remaining. As the tornado tracked west of Wellman, Iowa it knocked over a 325 ft cell phone tower. The tornado moved  into extreme southeast Iowa County near the intersection of Iowa-Washington Road and Ginkgo Ave., and then into far southwest Johnson County, about a third of a mile north of the intersection of Iowa-Washington Road and Johnson Iowa Road SW. The tornado was rated EF-4 in Keokuk County, EF-3 in Washington County, and EF-1 in Iowa and Johnson Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Excessive Heat Warning was in effect for portions of the forecast area for 7 days as a heat wave dominated the area. This was the longest duration Excessive Heat Warning that the Omaha office has issued. A total of 10 temperature records at Omaha, Lincoln, and Norfolk were tied or broken as the air temperature routinely topped 100�� and overnight low temperatures largely remained above 75��. High dew points led to heat indices that routinely topped 110��.||The heat wave began on August 19 as a mid-level ridge moved over the central CONUS. Temperatures in southwest Iowa ranged from the mid 90s and reached 100 at the Council Bluffs airport. Heat indices topped 120�� for several days as dewpoint temperatures were near 80��. A cold front moved into the area on August 20, but only brought a brief relief. Overnight low temperatures the morning of August 20 largely remained above 75 degrees. On August 21, afternoon highs returned to the 90s and heat indices easily topped 110��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For 7 days, the high temperature was above 90�� for all but August 20 and August 25. The highest observed temperature was 100�� on August 24. The peak heat index reached at least 100�� each day. The highest calculated heat index was a 114�� on August 21. The morning low temperatures never dropped below 72�� during the heat wave. The morning low temperature August 24 was 81��.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arc of convection developed ahead of a slow-moving upper-level low across central Nebraska. With around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE in a narrow zone and around 40 knots of deep layer shear, a few rotating severe storms developed with enhanced wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous 1 to 2 inch diameter tree branches, as well as one limb up to 4 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tail end of large scale forcing associated with an upper-level low moving across North Dakota brushed through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa just after diurnal heating maximum. With a weak moisture convergence axis and 500-1000 J/kg MLCAPE in the presence of 40 knots of deep-layer shear, isolated multicell storms developed which produced marginally large hail during the very brief life cycle.

A weakening upper level low pressure area moved into far southeast South Dakota by early evening. Slow moving thunderstorms developed within a very narrow axis which nosed into Buena Vista County, where the environment featured maximized and collocated 0-3km shear and 0-3km CAPE, and resulted in a few brief tornadoes connected with storm development phase.  In addition, very efficient thermodynamic profiles resulted in extremely heavy rainfall rates, with observed rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser video captured a very brief touchdown in an open field. No damage indicators were impacted. Therefore, an EF rating could not be determined.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 3 miles north of Atalissa, Iowa in Cedar County, snapping power poles and large trees along its path. It caused significant damage to two homes southwest of Bennett, Iowa, and flipped over a semi along interstate 80. It also damaged several concrete block silos south of Bennett. The tornado continued to track to the northeast through a rural area, causing sporadic tree damage.  The tornado entered extreme northwest Scott County about a third of a mile south of 220th St., then travelled around one mile before entering Clinton County about a half mile west of the intersection of 280th St. and 110th Ave. The tornado then dissipated about 2 miles south of Wheatland. The peak winds were estimated at 120 mph. The tornado was rated EF-2 in Cedar County, and EF-0 in Scott and Clinton Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening. After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage was done by strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large violent tornado developed in Wapello County (see NWS Des Moines Storm Data) and tracked into Keokuk County about 1 mile southeast of Martinsburg on Wapello-Keokuk Road. The tornado continued to the northeast before lifting just north of Highway 92, southwest of Keota. The tornado produced EF-3 damage to a house northeast of Martinsburg, and caused additional significant damage to farmsteads and outbuildings. The tornado was on the ground in Keokuk County for about 17 miles, with a maximum width of 1000 yards and peak estimated winds of 150 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening. After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon along a trailing boundary which extended from a subtle upper-level impulse that lifted from South Dakota into North Dakota.  A couple stronger cells in the line produced some penny- to nickel-sized hail.

An arc of convection developed ahead of a slow-moving upper-level low across central Nebraska. With around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE in a narrow zone and around 40 knots of deep layer shear, a few rotating severe storms developed with enhanced wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several medium-sized limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong tornado developed over the southeast part of Grand Mound, Iowa, and tracked through Charlotte, Iowa. A continuous damage track was noted, with several damaged houses, especially in Charlotte. The highest damage rating was located northeast of Grand Mound, where a whole house was shifted off it's foundation, and an entire house completely collapsed. Many areas noted tree damage and snapped power poles along the track of the tornado as well. Damage was also noted in Charlotte, associated with partial to complete destruction of barns and outbuildings. 3 people where trapped in the house that collapsed in Grand Mound, and one was transported to the hospital with minor injuries. The peak winds were estimated at 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening upper level low pressure area moved into far southeast South Dakota by early evening. Slow moving thunderstorms developed within a very narrow axis which nosed into Buena Vista County, where the environment featured maximized and collocated 0-3km shear and 0-3km CAPE, and resulted in a few brief tornadoes connected with storm development phase.  In addition, very efficient thermodynamic profiles resulted in extremely heavy rainfall rates, with observed rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A basement wall collapsed due to heavy rainfall and resultant flooding. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado tracked east across the southern portions of the town of Mediapolis, destroying siding and shingles on a few homes and businesses. Numerous large tree limbs fell on mobile homes and frame houses, damaging roofs and siding. In the middle of town, at it's peak strength, the tornado widened to 150 yards and produced EF2 damage at a home, where it removed the entire roof, and snapped a few large trees off just above the ground. The tornado dissipated quickly on the east side of town. The peak winds are estimated to be 120 mph with this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Excessive Heat Warning was in effect for portions of the forecast area for 7 days as a heat wave dominated the area. This was the longest duration Excessive Heat Warning that the Omaha office has issued. A total of 10 temperature records at Omaha, Lincoln, and Norfolk were tied or broken as the air temperature routinely topped 100�� and overnight low temperatures largely remained above 75��. High dew points led to heat indices that routinely topped 110��.||The heat wave began on August 19 as a mid-level ridge moved over the central CONUS. Temperatures in southwest Iowa ranged from the mid 90s and reached 100 at the Council Bluffs airport. Heat indices topped 120�� for several days as dewpoint temperatures were near 80��. A cold front moved into the area on August 20, but only brought a brief relief. Overnight low temperatures the morning of August 20 largely remained above 75 degrees. On August 21, afternoon highs returned to the 90s and heat indices easily topped 110��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beginning on August 19, the high temperature at the Clarinda AWOS reached at least 93�� each day through August 25 except for August 20. The highest observed temperature was 97��, occurring on both August 24 and August 25. The peak heat index reached at least 110�� each day. The highest calculated heat index was a 120��, occurring on both August 21 and August 23.

Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another large, violent tornado developed in eastern Keokuk County as the initial tornado was weakening southwest of Keota. The tornado tracked to the northeast, on the western fringe of Keota, and crossed into Washington County near the intersection of Keokuk-Washington Road and 180th St. The tornado then continued through northwest Washington county, extreme southeast Iowa County, and into far southwest Johnson County. The tornado severely damaged several homes near Keota, wiping one house completely off its foundation, resulting in EF-4 level damage. A car was lofted in the air and tossed about 1000 feet into a nearby field and trees were completely debarked with only stubs of the largest branches remaining. As the tornado tracked west of Wellman, Iowa it knocked over a 325 ft cell phone tower. The tornado moved  into extreme southeast Iowa County near the intersection of Iowa-Washington Road and Ginkgo Ave., and then into far southwest Johnson County, about a third of a mile north of the intersection of Iowa-Washington Road and Johnson Iowa Road SW. The tornado dissipated just northwest of Hillcrest Academy in Johnson County. The tornado was rated EF-4 in Keokuk County, EF-3 in Washington County, and EF-1 in Iowa and Johnson Counties.

As the tornado neared the Johnson-Linn county line, many trees were snapped or uprooted, with a few rolling through the part of the adjacent field. A house sustained minor shingle damage and broken windows. The tornado crossed into Linn County about a quarter of a mile north of the intersection of 110th St. NE and Taft Ave. NE. The tornado was rated EF2 in Johnson County with maximum winds around 130 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed in west central Iowa shortly after midnight on July 17th, developing on the nose of mid level theta-e advection and associated surface front lagging behind. Despite the timing, the environment recovered thanks to continued warm air advection, effective CAPE of over 1700 J/kg, and deep layer shear over 50 kts. Storms refired around 5 am, producing up to quarter sized hail as cells moved across southern Iowa through mid morning.

Early afternoon thunderstorms moved from east to west over locations near the Mississippi River and westward. While these were intense storms, reports of large hail were less than one inch in diameter in Iowa, but a few storms did produce damaging downburst winds.

Severe drought conditions persisted across portions of northeast Iowa into the beginning of July. Relief from the ongoing severe drought was realized following a rain event on July 4th and 5th, when generally 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rainfall was recorded across the area. Maximum two day rainfall amounts included 2.18 inches near Waucoma (Fayette County), 1.51 inches near Fort Atkinson (Winneshiek County), and 1.4 inches in New Hampton (Chickasaw County). This rainfall resulted in a temporary suspension of severe drought conditions across all of northeast Iowa with the July 18th Drought Monitor issuance. However, dry conditions thereafter led to severe drought conditions returning to eastern Allamakee County late in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in severe drought conditions for 574 square miles or 78% of Fayette County persisting into the middle of July. Increased rainfall of 2 to 4 inches according to volunteer observers resulted in some drought improvement across Fayette County with the July 18th Drought Monitor issuance, with severe drought conditions reverting to moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An immense ridge of high pressure over the Central Plains and Mississippi Valley entrenched heat and high dewpoint air across the region, resulting in daily heat indices from 100 to around 120, with very little recovery during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 103 to 116 degrees with a maximum heat index of 116 degrees at an Automated Surface Observing System site near Spencer on August 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sank south across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of July 28th. The environment was primed for severe storms with warm moist air in place; as temperatures climbed into the 90s and dewpoints into the 70s. By afternoon MLCAPE values of 3000 J/kg were in place across much of Iowa with deep layer shear exceeding 40 kts. Such an environment supported rapid storm development along the boundary by late afternoon. Storms produced ping-pong ball to baseball sized hail along with two tornadoes, damaging confinement pens and creating easily identifiable tracks on high resolution satellite imagery through surrounding fields. As storms pushed south into the evening, the hazards transitioned to a wind threat. A micro burst occurred in Marshall County very near the ASOS, resulting in an isolated pocket of 80 to 90 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a rather slow moving tornado which moved east initially through open cropland.  The tornado then turned southeast, east and eventually northeast as it journeyed through the farmland.  The tornado did hit a hog confinement with the roof collapsing producing EF1 damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms tracked across southeast Iowa during the mid-afternoon into early evening of June 24, producing large hail up to golf ball size in Van Buren County and tennis ball size hail near Ottumwa.||More organized clusters of severe storms tracked through Dubuque into far northwest IL during the evening and late night hours of June 24.  Another storm cluster moved through the Quad Cities, producing wind gusts to 70 mph at the Davenport ASOS and damage to trees and power poles.  An EF-0 tornado also tracked east along and north of I-80 near the Davenport Airport, causing sporadic minor tree and structure damage.  This storm impacted the Quad City Airshow grounds, while no visitors were present.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expanding upper-level ridge across the southern and central U.S. pushed heat into the region, as moisture pooled just ahead of an advancing frontal zone. The combination of heat and higher dewpoints resulted in afternoon and early evening heat indices from 100 to 115 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum heat index of 108 degrees was reached at the Automated Weather Observing System site KCKP near Cherokee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of convection moved across Iowa in quick succession beginning in the pre-dawn hours of July 12th. This first was an area of convection that developed along an elevated boundary into northwest Iowa. While this area of storms mainly remained sub-severe, severe hail did fall across central Iowa near 6 am. At the same time a MCS moved out of the Dakotas and into western Iowa, moving quickly and eventually merging with the first area of storms. This second round developed into a line of storms with winds gusting up to 70 mph, fueled by the low level jet. Orientation of 0-3 km shear was favorable for quick spin-ups and two weaker tornadoes did form in Cass county that morning. With deep warm cloud layers over 4000 meters and precipitable water nearing 2, areas that received rain in both rounds received two to four inches by the time storms ended late in the morning. An isolated pocket of four to six inches fell in Greene and Boone counties, creating flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado was indicated by a Correlation Coefficient depression on radar and also verified through high resolution satellite imagery north of Marne.  The tornado remained in mostly open cropland although some EF0 tree damage was done to a tree line along the edge of one of the fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sank south across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of July 28th. The environment was primed for severe storms with warm moist air in place; as temperatures climbed into the 90s and dewpoints into the 70s. By afternoon MLCAPE values of 3000 J/kg were in place across much of Iowa with deep layer shear exceeding 40 kts. Such an environment supported rapid storm development along the boundary by late afternoon. Storms produced ping-pong ball to baseball sized hail along with two tornadoes, damaging confinement pens and creating easily identifiable tracks on high resolution satellite imagery through surrounding fields. As storms pushed south into the evening, the hazards transitioned to a wind threat. A micro burst occurred in Marshall County very near the ASOS, resulting in an isolated pocket of 80 to 90 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via mping.

A strong ridge of high pressure persisted through the Central and Southern Plains at the start of September and brought hot temperatures to the region with highs in the 90s to around 100 degrees. Dewpoint readings were somewhat lower than late August, mainly in the 60s, which kept the more extreme heat at bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a two-day period from the afternoon of September 2 through early evening on September 3, a maximum heat index of 101 degrees was reached at Automated Weather Observing System site KSXK near Maurice and 100 degrees at Iowa Road Weather Information System site RTNI4 near Alton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions persisted across portions of northeast Iowa into the beginning of July. Relief from the ongoing severe drought was realized following a rain event on July 4th and 5th, when generally 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rainfall was recorded across the area. Maximum two day rainfall amounts included 2.18 inches near Waucoma (Fayette County), 1.51 inches near Fort Atkinson (Winneshiek County), and 1.4 inches in New Hampton (Chickasaw County). This rainfall resulted in a temporary suspension of severe drought conditions across all of northeast Iowa with the July 18th Drought Monitor issuance. However, dry conditions thereafter led to severe drought conditions returning to eastern Allamakee County late in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in severe drought conditions for 283 square miles across southern Floyd County persisting into the middle of July. Increased rainfall of 1 to 2 inches according to volunteer observers resulted in some drought improvement across Floyd County with the July 18th Drought Monitor issuance, with the severe drought conditions being removed from the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in severe drought conditions for 387 square miles of eastern Allamakee County persisting into the middle of July. Increased rainfall of 2 to 4 inches according to volunteer observers resulted in a temporary removal of severe drought conditions from Allamakee County with the July 18th Drought Monitor issuance. Severe drought conditions returned for 264 square miles across eastern Allamakee County by late July due to the return of drier than normal conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from earlier day convection focused a gradient of instability and storm development with additional destabilization from heating by early afternoon. Boundary interactions from sluggish storms led to training of heavy rainfall cores, as well as a couple brief tornadoes. Extensive flooding resulted in a state disaster declaration for Sioux County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust to 71 mph was measured by a law enforcement officer.  Some minor damage to shingles and trees was observed near the point the wind was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of convection moved across Iowa in quick succession beginning in the pre-dawn hours of July 12th. This first was an area of convection that developed along an elevated boundary into northwest Iowa. While this area of storms mainly remained sub-severe, severe hail did fall across central Iowa near 6 am. At the same time a MCS moved out of the Dakotas and into western Iowa, moving quickly and eventually merging with the first area of storms. This second round developed into a line of storms with winds gusting up to 70 mph, fueled by the low level jet. Orientation of 0-3 km shear was favorable for quick spin-ups and two weaker tornadoes did form in Cass county that morning. With deep warm cloud layers over 4000 meters and precipitable water nearing 2, areas that received rain in both rounds received two to four inches by the time storms ended late in the morning. An isolated pocket of four to six inches fell in Greene and Boone counties, creating flash flooding in the area.

Evening thunderstorms focused along a weak surface boundary from northwest Iowa into areas south of Interstate 90 in southeast South Dakota. Even with very weak deep-layer shear, modest surface-based instability of 1500-2000 J/kg led to the rapid development of storms, which peaked in mainly hail producing severity early in the life cycle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early afternoon thunderstorms moved from east to west over locations near the Mississippi River and westward. While these were intense storms, reports of large hail were less than one inch in diameter in Iowa, but a few storms did produce damaging downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal chimney was blown off a house, a mailbox damaged, and several large tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms tracked across southeast Iowa during the mid-afternoon into early evening of June 24, producing large hail up to golf ball size in Van Buren County and tennis ball size hail near Ottumwa.||More organized clusters of severe storms tracked through Dubuque into far northwest IL during the evening and late night hours of June 24.  Another storm cluster moved through the Quad Cities, producing wind gusts to 70 mph at the Davenport ASOS and damage to trees and power poles.  An EF-0 tornado also tracked east along and north of I-80 near the Davenport Airport, causing sporadic minor tree and structure damage.  This storm impacted the Quad City Airshow grounds, while no visitors were present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report included a video and photos on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expanding upper-level ridge across the southern and central U.S. pushed heat into the region, as moisture pooled just ahead of an advancing frontal zone. The combination of heat and higher dewpoints resulted in afternoon and early evening heat indices from 100 to 115 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum heat index of 109 degrees was reached at the Automated Weather Observing System site KSLB near Storm Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening. After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half-dollar size hail was accompanied by some strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite temperatures which averaged 2 to 5 degrees above normal during May, near to somewhat above normal precipitation alleviated, at least to a small degree, drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) conditions by mid-May. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms developed in the exit region of a strong jet streak in northwest flow aloft, focused along a prefrontal trough as it moved from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa. Storms quickly became severe, with MLCAPE of up to 1500 J/kg and deep layer shear around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from earlier day convection focused a gradient of instability and storm development with additional destabilization from heating by early afternoon. Boundary interactions from sluggish storms led to training of heavy rainfall cores, as well as a couple brief tornadoes. Extensive flooding resulted in a state disaster declaration for Sioux County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A highly visible tornado developed a quarter mile south of Iowa Highway 10 just to the east of Goldfinch Avenue. The slow-moving tornado was photographed by several members of the public as it tracked generally a bit south of due eastward.  Very soon after touching down, the tornado impacted a shelter belt where it did damage to several hardwood trees.  After a quarter mile, the tornado track shifted slightly toward the east-northeast across open farmland. The tornado crossed Grant Avenue and lifted shortly thereafter.  The average path width was 25 yards. The tornado was rated an EF-0 with a maximum wind speed of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sank south across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of July 28th. The environment was primed for severe storms with warm moist air in place; as temperatures climbed into the 90s and dewpoints into the 70s. By afternoon MLCAPE values of 3000 J/kg were in place across much of Iowa with deep layer shear exceeding 40 kts. Such an environment supported rapid storm development along the boundary by late afternoon. Storms produced ping-pong ball to baseball sized hail along with two tornadoes, damaging confinement pens and creating easily identifiable tracks on high resolution satellite imagery through surrounding fields. As storms pushed south into the evening, the hazards transitioned to a wind threat. A micro burst occurred in Marshall County very near the ASOS, resulting in an isolated pocket of 80 to 90 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud north of Denver, Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved over Iowa, Missouri, and western Illinois the afternoon of June 2nd. A few of these produced localized downburst winds. Burlington Iowa saw a strong wind gust from one of these storms, that reached severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Burlington Iowa's airport reported a severe wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Excessive Heat Warning was in effect for portions of the forecast area for 7 days as a heat wave dominated the area. This was the longest duration Excessive Heat Warning that the Omaha office has issued. A total of 10 temperature records at Omaha, Lincoln, and Norfolk were tied or broken as the air temperature routinely topped 100�� and overnight low temperatures largely remained above 75��. High dew points led to heat indices that routinely topped 110��.||The heat wave began on August 19 as a mid-level ridge moved over the central CONUS. Temperatures in southwest Iowa ranged from the mid 90s and reached 100 at the Council Bluffs airport. Heat indices topped 120�� for several days as dewpoint temperatures were near 80��. A cold front moved into the area on August 20, but only brought a brief relief. Overnight low temperatures the morning of August 20 largely remained above 75 degrees. On August 21, afternoon highs returned to the 90s and heat indices easily topped 110��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beginning on August 19, the high temperature at the Red Oak AWOS reached at least 93�� each day through August 25 except for August 20. The highest observed temperature was 99�� on August 24. The peak heat index reached at least 105�� each day. The highest calculated heat index was a 122�� on August 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple funnel clouds were reported as a surface low moved across Iowa. This supplied enhanced vorticity in a region of weak shear and pockets of increased low level instability, allowing for a prime environment for enhanced low level stretching for funnel development. No funnels developed into tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud reported on social media, with image.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from earlier day convection focused a gradient of instability and storm development with additional destabilization from heating by early afternoon. Boundary interactions from sluggish storms led to training of heavy rainfall cores, as well as a couple brief tornadoes. Extensive flooding resulted in a state disaster declaration for Sioux County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several yards flooded due to a small creek overflowing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms focused along a weak surface boundary from northwest Iowa into areas south of Interstate 90 in southeast South Dakota. Even with very weak deep-layer shear, modest surface-based instability of 1500-2000 J/kg led to the rapid development of storms, which peaked in mainly hail producing severity early in the life cycle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Row crops were badly damaged by hail up to golf ball size. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm developed in the axis of greatest elevated instability around 1000 J/kg CAPE and dropped occasional large hail moving southeast across northwest Iowa.

Multiple funnel clouds were reported as a surface low moved across Iowa. This supplied enhanced vorticity in a region of weak shear and pockets of increased low level instability, allowing for a prime environment for enhanced low level stretching for funnel development. No funnels developed into tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud reported on by trained spotter east of Rockwell City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple funnel clouds were reported as a surface low moved across Iowa. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief funnel cloud reported by spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms percolated through much of the day along a stationary boundary over northwest Iowa. By evening a more robust shortwave moved across the area, accompanied by 30 kts of shear and 1500-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE. This allowed for storms to strengthen across west central Iowa with gusty winds producing some minor damage to trees and out buildings. Additionally, due to the orientation of the shear, a few weak tornadoes developed and were discovered using high resolution satellite imagery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado occurred north of Highway 30 west of Carroll Iowa.  The tornado was found using high resolution satellite imagery.   The tornado was brief and did not impact any damage indicators, hence the EFU rating.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water ponding in the area of Central Park overflowed roadways and entered the Sioux Center Fire Department building. Two homes suffered the collapse of basement walls from the high water. Water also inundated a transformer which cut off power to seven homes in the northwest part of town. Seven trailers were evacuated due to high water in Westbrook Village on the northwest edge of town. More than two dozen vehicles were stranded in high water. Damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of convection moved across Iowa in quick succession beginning in the pre-dawn hours of July 12th. This first was an area of convection that developed along an elevated boundary into northwest Iowa. While this area of storms mainly remained sub-severe, severe hail did fall across central Iowa near 6 am. At the same time a MCS moved out of the Dakotas and into western Iowa, moving quickly and eventually merging with the first area of storms. This second round developed into a line of storms with winds gusting up to 70 mph, fueled by the low level jet. Orientation of 0-3 km shear was favorable for quick spin-ups and two weaker tornadoes did form in Cass county that morning. With deep warm cloud layers over 4000 meters and precipitable water nearing 2, areas that received rain in both rounds received two to four inches by the time storms ended late in the morning. An isolated pocket of four to six inches fell in Greene and Boone counties, creating flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowing through the grounds of Boone Speedway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave moved across northern and eastern Iowa. By peak afternoon heating, thunderstorms began developing along the boundary in northeast Iowa into southern Minnesota.  DCAPE approached 900 J/kg, supporting gusty winds with storms that developed. At the same time, deep layer shear over 40 knots and steep lapse rates supported hail growth, with several reports of quarter sized hail as the storms moved southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter sized hail. Relayed by Law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms percolated through much of the day along a stationary boundary over northwest Iowa. By evening a more robust shortwave moved across the area, accompanied by 30 kts of shear and 1500-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE. This allowed for storms to strengthen across west central Iowa with gusty winds producing some minor damage to trees and out buildings. Additionally, due to the orientation of the shear, a few weak tornadoes developed and were discovered using high resolution satellite imagery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado was found using high resolution satellite imagery.  Tornado had a curved path through open agricultural fields.  There was no damage to damage indicators, hence the EFU rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expanding upper-level ridge across the southern and central U.S. pushed heat into the region, as moisture pooled just ahead of an advancing frontal zone. The combination of heat and higher dewpoints resulted in afternoon and early evening heat indices from 100 to 115 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum heat index of 115 degrees was reached at RAWS site BKTI4 3 miles south-southeast of Westfield and 111 degrees at Automated Weather Observing System site KLRJ near Le Mars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave moved across northern and eastern Iowa. By peak afternoon heating, thunderstorms began developing along the boundary in northeast Iowa into southern Minnesota. Convection developed in west central Iowa shortly after midnight on July 17th, developing on the nose of mid level theta-e advection and associated surface front lagging behind. Despite the timing, the environment recovered thanks to continued warm air advection, effective CAPE of over 1700 J/kg, and deep layer shear over 50 kts. Storms refired around 5 am, producing up to quarter sized hail as cells moved across southern Iowa through mid morning.
