EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the latter half of the afternoon along a slow moving cold front.  Dry air poised off the surface allowed for outflow dominant storms to develop, with a brief period of damaging gusty wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An older tree was blown down across the road near the intersection of Dogwood Trail and Port Neal Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions persisted across portions of northeast Iowa into the beginning of July. Relief from the ongoing severe drought was realized following a rain event on July 4th and 5th, when generally 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rainfall was recorded across the area. Maximum two day rainfall amounts included 2.18 inches near Waucoma (Fayette County), 1.51 inches near Fort Atkinson (Winneshiek County), and 1.4 inches in New Hampton (Chickasaw County). This rainfall resulted in a temporary suspension of severe drought conditions across all of northeast Iowa with the July 18th Drought Monitor issuance. However, dry conditions thereafter led to severe drought conditions returning to eastern Allamakee County late in the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in severe drought conditions for 686 square miles or 87% of Clayton County through the middle of July. Increased rainfall totaling 1.23 to 4.85 inches according to volunteer observers resulted in some drought improvement across Clayton County with the July 18th Drought Monitor issuance, with severe drought conditions reverting to moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An immense ridge of high pressure over the Central Plains and Mississippi Valley entrenched heat and high dewpoint air across the region, resulting in daily heat indices from 100 to around 120, with very little recovery during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 106 to 116 degrees with a maximum heat index of 116 degrees at an Automated Weather Observing System site at Cherokee on August 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening upper level low pressure area moved into far southeast South Dakota by early evening. Slow moving thunderstorms developed within a very narrow axis which nosed into Buena Vista County, where the environment featured maximized and collocated 0-3km shear and 0-3km CAPE, and resulted in a few brief tornadoes connected with storm development phase.  In addition, very efficient thermodynamic profiles resulted in extremely heavy rainfall rates, with observed rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rural roads south of Albert City were inundated by water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from earlier day convection focused a gradient of instability and storm development with additional destabilization from heating by early afternoon. Boundary interactions from sluggish storms led to training of heavy rainfall cores, as well as a couple brief tornadoes. Extensive flooding resulted in a state disaster declaration for Sioux County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads impassible around Rock Valley due to very high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant rain event occurred late Friday into Saturday across extreme southeast Iowa.  A slow moving mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) tracked over eastern Lee and Des Moines Counties and brought very heavy rain in a short amount of time.  Rainfall amounts between 3 and 9 inches fell in less than 6 hours, which caused many stalled vehicles and several water rescues in Keokuk, Iowa.  Other towns that received heavy rain were Fort Madison, Iowa and Burlington, Iowa.||A new daily rainfall record of 6.14 of rain occurred in Burlington, which broke the previous record of 2.03 back in 1903.  This record also is the second highest daily rainfall on any given day in Burlington.  The wettest day was 6.28 on June 29th, 1933.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported several stalled vehicles and water rescues by first responders in town in the past 2 to 3 hours. There was 4 to 5 feet of water reported on many roads and streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Linear storms developed near a frontal boundary, moving across southeast South Dakota and into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota early on June 24. Within an environment of shear, a strong low-level jet, and strong DCAPE, some storms produced severe-level winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed several trees and blew shingles off of a roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Effective shear values of 30 to 40 kts with weakly capped elevated CAPE on the order of 1000 J/kg allowed for storms to develop on the leading edge of warm advection with retreat of an upper-level ridge.  Stronger storms produced one- to two-inch hail.

A strong cold front moved into the area during the morning and afternoon hours. A lake breeze boundary also moved into the area from the east. Thunderstorms interacted with this boundary in Dubuque County that resulted in several funnel cloud reports and one of these funnels touching down in a farm field near Cascade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Iowa on the evening of May 6, 2023. Storms mainly produced hail up to golf ball size. Through the evening, storms merged and weakened in intensity, but not before producing some wind damage across northern and central Lee County. An isolated tornado also occurred in Muscatine County. Heavy rain also fell from storms as some remained stationary for long periods of time. Most notably, locations north and east of Fairfield, Iowa recorded over two inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Burlington Airport measured a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms impacted the area on Sunday May 7, 2023.  Two areas of storms were present during the mid to late afternoon, one entering the Cedar Rapids and Iowa City metros and another around the Burlington and Fort Madison metros. The first was the most severe, producing hail up to baseball size in Koszta, Iowa and widespread wind damage, especially around West Liberty, Iowa. This storm, and storms to the west, eventually merged into a cluster of severe thunderstorms, producing large hail and destructive winds around 70-75 mph all the way to Mount Pleasant, Iowa and Macomb, Illinois. The latter produced sporadic reports of quarter to half dollar sized hail, along with a brief landspout tornado east of Yorktown, IL.  There was also severe blowing dust which was especially bad near I-55 in north central Illinois. Even more thunderstorms developed overnight across the area, and mainly produced torrential rain and isolated large hail reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Iowa on the evening of May 6, 2023. Storms mainly produced hail up to golf ball size. Through the evening, storms merged and weakened in intensity, but not before producing some wind damage across northern and central Lee County. An isolated tornado also occurred in Muscatine County. Heavy rain also fell from storms as some remained stationary for long periods of time. Most notably, locations north and east of Fairfield, Iowa recorded over two inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof torn off of a manufactured home due to the thunderstorm wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed just NNW of West Liberty and tracked across the far northeast part of town before eventually crossing over U.S. Highway 6 between West Liberty and Atalissa, and diminishing shortly after. A few large trees and two utility poles were also snapped. A large metal batting cage anchored into concrete was twisted and bent sharply at its base.

Two areas of storms were present during the mid to late afternoon, one entering the Cedar Rapids and Iowa City metros and another around the Burlington and Fort Madison metros. The first was the most severe, producing hail up to baseball size in Koszta, Iowa and widespread wind damage, especially around West Liberty, Iowa. This storm, and storms to the west, eventually merged into a cluster of severe thunderstorms, producing large hail and destructive winds around 70-75 mph all the way to Mount Pleasant, Iowa and Macomb, Illinois. The latter produced sporadic reports of quarter to half dollar sized hail, along with a brief landspout tornado east of Yorktown, IL.  There was also severe blowing dust which was especially bad near I-55 in north central Illinois. Even more thunderstorms developed overnight across the area, and mainly produced torrential rain and isolated large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 63 mph wind gust with thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Iowa on the evening of May 6, 2023. Storms mainly produced hail up to golf ball size. Through the evening, storms merged and weakened in intensity, but not before producing some wind damage across northern and central Lee County. An isolated tornado also occurred in Muscatine County. Heavy rain also fell from storms as some remained stationary for long periods of time. Most notably, locations north and east of Fairfield, Iowa recorded over two inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail, with very strong winds that resulted in some minor damage to the trees.

Drought maintained its presence across central Iowa during October despite some spotty rainfall.  The longer term dryness allowed any rain to soak in quickly with little response to area streams or rivers.  Severe to Extreme Drought persisted although some improvement was seen in northwest Iowa where precipitation for the month was over 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditons maintained a presence in Sac county during the month of October.  Temperatures remained above normal during the month with near normal rainfall although longer range precipitation remains well below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal teperatures through the fall along with precipitation remaining at or below normal for much of the area allowed drought to persist through November.  The overall area of severe to extreme drought remained very stable for much of the month with only some minor expansion in the center part of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to Extreme drought conditions persisted in Worth county for the duration of November given lack of rain and above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite multiple rainfall events through October, there was not much change to overall drought severity. Monthly rainfall totals of 1 to 4 inches ranged from slightly below normal in Mitchell, Howard, Floyd, and Chickasaw counties to slightly above normal in Winneshiek, Fayette, Clayton, and Allamakee counties. The drought improved slightly in far northeast Iowa from extreme (D3) to severe (D2) by the end of October. Elsewhere, while ongoing below normal soil moisture became 10 to 20 percent above normal through the middle of the month, the long-standing drier than normal conditions resulted in extreme drought (D3) persisting. From the start of June through September, near 10 inches of total rainfall was 6 to 10 inches below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long-standing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 6 consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2), resulted in continuation of extreme drought (D3) across Howard County through October. While monthly rainfall totals near 2 inches were only an inch below normal, the long standing deficit resulted in the extreme drought (D3) persisting. The ongoing rainfall deficit from initial severe drought (D2) issuance on August 15th to October 1st was 2 to 4 inches below normal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail fell for 10 minutes.

A broad area of low pressure moved slowly across the state through the day, with a number of associated weak boundaries that resulted in off and on convection through the day. In the afternoon the primary boundary, acting almost as a dry line, was draped across Iowa from northwest to southeast. With good 0-3 km CAPE exceeding 124 J/kg and surface vorticity to enhance stretching in the vicinity of the surface low, conditions were good for funnel clouds and tornadoes. As the band of thunderstorms moved slowly across central Iowa it produced two weak tornadoes along with three funnel clouds.  One tornado was rated EFU as it remained in rural farmland and the other was an EF0 with minimal damage produced to a tin building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak rotation captured on video near the Iowa Speedway. The weak circulation moved north across Interstate 80 and dissipated over East 36 street south of Iowa Speedway Drive. Very minor damage was done to the top of a building with a small section of tin removed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure spread a tight surface pressure gradient over the region, and winds were briefly enhanced while precipitation moved through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Synoptic wind gusts up to 58 mph were measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KSXK near Maurice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought maintained its presence across central Iowa during October despite some spotty rainfall.  The longer term dryness allowed any rain to soak in quickly with little response to area streams or rivers.  Severe to Extreme Drought persisted although some improvement was seen in northwest Iowa where precipitation for the month was over 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe  to extreme drought conditons maintained a presence in Worth county during the month of October.  Temperatures remained above normal during the month with near normal rainfall although longer range precipitation remains well below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-level jet and subtle mid-level wave supported isolated convective development, with up to 1000 J/kg of elevated CAPE across northwest Iowa. The strongest cells produced some hail up to quarter size.

A strong low-level jet and subtle mid-level wave supported isolated convective development, with up to 1000 J/kg of elevated CAPE across northwest Iowa. The strongest cells produced some hail up to quarter size.

A continuation of above normal temperatures and lack of rainfall contributed to a deterioration of drought conditions in additional portions of central Iowa with Severe Drought contitions widening. This was an expansion of a much larger area of severe or worse drought conditions over a large portions of Iowa. Agricultural crops were mainly unaffected by this point in the season as many of the crops had already reached maturity. However, the drier conditions did hasten the dry down of crops with harvest in full swing by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and lack of precipitation allowed severe drought conditions to expand into portions of Ringgold county during the later half of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with an associated surface low pressure system pushed into the state on the afternoon of September 23rd. Favorable instability and bulk shear present in the atmosphere allowed for strong to severe storms, which produced some sub-severe to severe hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from mPING of quarter (1 inch) hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions resulted in extreme drought (D3) encompassing nearly all of northeastern Iowa at the start of September. Highly variable monthly rainfall totals, ranging from 1 to 6 inches, mostly fell during the latter half of the month. With a very dry start to the month, rainfall totals neared 2 inches below normal, resulting in exceptional drought (D4) for far northeast Iowa. Broadcast media reported heightened drought impacts across northeast Iowa compared to neighboring areas, resulting in stunted and below average crop yields. A series of storms during the latter half of the month provided heavy rain across northeast Iowa, easing the single week of exceptional drought (D4). Highest recorded 24-hour rainfall totals, reported on the morning of the 23rd, included 3.26 near Decorah (Winneshiek), 3.25 in New Hampton (Chickasaw County), and 3.15 in Waukon (Allamakee County). This slightly reduced drought severity, but nevertheless extreme drought (D3) conditions persisted through the remainder of September for nearly all of northeast Iowa. This brought drought longevity to 7 (Howard County) to 15 (Allamakee County, Clayton County) consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing extreme drought (D3), comprised of 7 consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2), worsened to exceptional drought (D4) for most of Allamakee County through the middle of September. By September 11th, under a quarter of an inch of rain fell across Allamakee County, more than 1.5 inches below normal for the time period. The resultant topsoil moistures neared 50% of normal. By the middle of the month, all of Allamakee County was enveloped in exceptional drought (D4). A series of storms provided 3 to 6 inches of rainfall during the second half of September, about 1 to 4 inches above normal for the time period. This above normal rainfall resulted in removal of exceptional drought (D4) by the end of September, but extreme drought (D3) persisted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with an associated surface low pressure system pushed into the state on the afternoon of September 23rd. Favorable instability and bulk shear present in the atmosphere allowed for strong to severe storms, which produced some sub-severe to severe hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of damage to an outbuilding. Time of damage was estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel size hail located one mile west of New Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions resulted in extreme drought (D3) encompassing nearly all of northeastern Iowa at the start of September. Highly variable monthly rainfall totals, ranging from 1 to 6 inches, mostly fell during the latter half of the month. With a very dry start to the month, rainfall totals neared 2 inches below normal, resulting in exceptional drought (D4) for far northeast Iowa. Broadcast media reported heightened drought impacts across northeast Iowa compared to neighboring areas, resulting in stunted and below average crop yields. A series of storms during the latter half of the month provided heavy rain across northeast Iowa, easing the single week of exceptional drought (D4). Highest recorded 24-hour rainfall totals, reported on the morning of the 23rd, included 3.26 near Decorah (Winneshiek), 3.25 in New Hampton (Chickasaw County), and 3.15 in Waukon (Allamakee County). This slightly reduced drought severity, but nevertheless extreme drought (D3) conditions persisted through the remainder of September for nearly all of northeast Iowa. This brought drought longevity to 7 (Howard County) to 15 (Allamakee County, Clayton County) consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing extreme drought (D3) persisted through Chickasaw County, worsening to exceptional drought (D4) in the northeast part of the county by mid-September. By September 11th, less than a quarter of an inch of rain had fallen across Chickasaw County, which was more than 1.25 inches below normal for the time period. The resultant topsoil moistures reached 50 to 70% below normal. Farmers reported very poor conditions in southwestern Chickasaw and an earlier than normal harvest time. A series of storms provided up to 5 inches of rainfall during the second half of September, nearing 3 inches above normal in spots for the time period. This above normal rainfall resulted in removal of exceptional drought (D4) by the end of September, while extreme drought (D3) persisted county-wide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing extreme drought (D3) persisted across all of Clayton County and worsened to exceptional drought (D4) across the northeast part of the county by mid-September. Highly variable rainfall of a tenth of inch in the north to 1 inch in the south of Clayton County through September 11th was 0.5 to 1.5 inches below normal during this time. The resultant topsoil moistures were 50% of normal. A series of storms provided 1 to 3 inches of rainfall across during the second half of September, up to 1 inch above normal for the time period. The above normal rainfall resulted in removal of exceptional drought (D4) by the end of September, replaced by persistent extreme (D3) and severe drought (D2). This marked 11 consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2) in Clayton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions resulted in extreme drought (D3) encompassing nearly all of northeastern Iowa at the start of September. Highly variable monthly rainfall totals, ranging from 1 to 6 inches, mostly fell during the latter half of the month. With a very dry start to the month, rainfall totals neared 2 inches below normal, resulting in exceptional drought (D4) for far northeast Iowa. Broadcast media reported heightened drought impacts across northeast Iowa compared to neighboring areas, resulting in stunted and below average crop yields. A series of storms during the latter half of the month provided heavy rain across northeast Iowa, easing the single week of exceptional drought (D4). Highest recorded 24-hour rainfall totals, reported on the morning of the 23rd, included 3.26 near Decorah (Winneshiek), 3.25 in New Hampton (Chickasaw County), and 3.15 in Waukon (Allamakee County). This slightly reduced drought severity, but nevertheless extreme drought (D3) conditions persisted through the remainder of September for nearly all of northeast Iowa. This brought drought longevity to 7 (Howard County) to 15 (Allamakee County, Clayton County) consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing extreme drought (D3) worsened to exceptional drought (D4) for nearly all of Winneshiek County through the middle of September. By September 11th, up to a quarter inch of rain fell across Winneshiek County, up to 1.5 inches below normal for the time period. The resultant topsoil moisture was 50 to 70% below normal. A series of storms provided 3 to 6 inches inches of rainfall during the second half of September, up to 3 inches above normal in spots during this time. This above normal rainfall resulted in removal of exceptional drought (D4) by the end of September, while extreme drought (D3) persisted county-wide.

However, the drier conditions did hasten the dry down of crops with harvest in full swing by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure passed through the area, bringing scattered showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms became strong to severe, producing small hail and gusty winds. Several reports of funnel clouds were received, with one weak tornado reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management confirmed a brief EF-U tornado several miles west of Vinton. Spotters reported a ground circulation lofting corn leaves, beneath the funnel. No damage was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms moved through the area, with one storm producing a weak tornado in Benton County, Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trained storm spotters reported a brief tornado. Location and time estimated by radar and reports. There were no reports of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure spread a tight surface pressure gradient over the region, and winds were briefly enhanced while precipitation moved through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Synoptic wind gusts up to 52 mph were measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KLRJ at Le Mars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying storm system sagged through northeast Iowa overnight from October 30th through October 31st. While maximum snow accumulations were only 1 to 2 inches for counties along the Minnesota border, the combination of strong winds and intense snow bands quickly resulted in slippery to hazardous road conditions. As the bands of snow quickly passed through the area, visibilities were reduced to less than a mile and resulted in numerous automobile accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While volunteer observers reported only 1 to 2 inches of snowfall from late on October 30th through the morning of October 31st, the combination with 40 mph wind gusts rapidly worsened road conditions. These slippery to hazardous travel conditions resulted in multiple slide offs reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite multiple rainfall events through October, there was not much change to overall drought severity. Monthly rainfall totals of 1 to 4 inches ranged from slightly below normal in Mitchell, Howard, Floyd, and Chickasaw counties to slightly above normal in Winneshiek, Fayette, Clayton, and Allamakee counties. The drought improved slightly in far northeast Iowa from extreme (D3) to severe (D2) by the end of October. Elsewhere, while ongoing below normal soil moisture became 10 to 20 percent above normal through the middle of the month, the long-standing drier than normal conditions resulted in extreme drought (D3) persisting. From the start of June through September, near 10 inches of total rainfall was 6 to 10 inches below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long-standing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 15 weeks of at least severe drought (D2), slightly eased in severity for Allamakee County through October. Several storms provided near normal monthly rainfall totals, easing initial extreme drought (D3) to severe drought (D2) for most of the county. Continuation of the severe drought (D2) was a result of the ongoing rainfall deficit near 6 inches from initial severe drought (D2) issuance on June 20th to the start of October.

A small area of Exceptional Drought developed in Tama county by the end of the month.   Agricultural crops were mainly unaffected by this point in the season as many of the crops had already reached maturity.  However, the drier conditions did hasten the dry down of crops with harvest in full swing by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought persisted in portions of Appanoose county during September given the above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July 28 was a hot day as temperatures in southwest Iowa peaked in the low 90s. Dewpoint temperatures rose into the high 70s and low 80s. This resulted in heat index values that peaked above 120��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harlan AWOS had a heat index at or above 110�� for approximately 5 hours. The peak heat index was 119��.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An enhanced risk resulted in a numerous hail event across southwest Iowa. Storms developed after 4 PM along a stationary front near the Nebraska/Kansas border and moved into Iowa. A total of 55 storm reports were sent out by the office, most of which were for hail. The largest hailstone reported was 3 inches near Thurman. In addition to the hail, there were 3 observed tornadoes. Two in Fremont County and one in Pottawatomie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 3-inch hail near Thurman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread significant damaging wind storm tracked across nearly all of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa on the morning of July 12. These storms produced widespread winds of 60 to 80 mph, with embedded stronger downbursts with wind speeds up to 100 mph. The strongest measured wind gust was 82 mph in Dodge County NE, but some of the high-end straight line wind damage supports embedded wind speeds up to 100 mph.||This storm also produced a few embedded tornadoes. Some of the tornado tracks were only identified by narrow swaths of damage through mature cornfields, with minimal damage otherwise and peak wind speeds similar to the nearby downburst winds. All but one of the tornadoes was preceded by a Wireless Emergency Alert. There were 0 reported injuries or fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There is a distinct tornado track through crop fields approximately 40 yards wide at the max. The track begins in a soybean field and then moves into the corn field where the narrow track and convergent flow pattern are most pronounced. It crosses a creek, breaks a few branches, and then through another corn field where there is convergent flow into the center of the track from the edges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-standing abnormally dry conditions from April 2023 through December resulted in no changes to severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought for northeast Iowa in December. From initial severe drought (D2) issuance in June 2023 to the onset of December, 10 to 15 of total rainfall was 8 to 14 below normal. December total rainfall was near 1 for most of northeast Iowa, outside of two select areas along and south of the Winneshiek and Allamakee county border where near 1.5 fell though the month. These higher amounts were near monthly normal while the rest of northeast Iowa was 0.5 to 1 below normal. Also, well above normal temperatures, 10 degrees above normal monthly average, perpetuated ongoing soil moisture concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to extreme drought (D3) persisted across Clayton County through December. While monthly total rainfall was near normal at 2, the long-standing abnormally dry conditions were 3 to 4 below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm impacted the Northern Plains in the days leading up to and through the Christmas holiday with widespread precipitation, wind, and hazardous conditions. The storm began as rain during the afternoon and evening of December 23, briefly pausing before a second round of rainfall surged northward early on December 24. Cooler temperatures slipped in near the surface by early Christmas Day, and this caused a widespread transition to freezing rain with ice accumulation varying from one-tenth to one quarter inch west of a Spencer to Ida Grove line. Cooler air aloft forced a change to accumulating snow around the Sioux City area on Christmas afternoon before precipitation ended. ||Several long-term NWS Cooperative Observer locations in northwest Iowa recorded the highest 3-day precipitation total on record for December including:||Remsen 2.14 |Orange City 1.62|Sibley 1.10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of light freezing rain produced ice accumulation up to two-tenths of an inch. The weight of ice on powerlines downed a power pole and resulted in several brief power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-standing abnormally dry conditions from April 2023 through December resulted in no changes to severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought for northeast Iowa in December. From initial severe drought (D2) issuance in June 2023 to the onset of December, 10 to 15 of total rainfall was 8 to 14 below normal. December total rainfall was near 1 for most of northeast Iowa, outside of two select areas along and south of the Winneshiek and Allamakee county border where near 1.5 fell though the month. These higher amounts were near monthly normal while the rest of northeast Iowa was 0.5 to 1 below normal. Also, well above normal temperatures, 10 degrees above normal monthly average, perpetuated ongoing soil moisture concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought persisted across all of Howard County through December. Monthly total rainfall near 0.5 was 1 below normal, exacerbating the long-standing deficit of 8 to 12  since June 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought conditions at the start of November remained constant across northeast Iowa through November from long-standing abnormally dry conditions. While the previous month of October provided near to slightly above normal rainfall amounts, total rainfall near 20 inches from April 1 to October 31st was 9 to 12 inches below normal. During November, only 1 to 3 days of measurable precipitation provided 0.1 to 0.5 inches total rainfall, near 2 inches below the monthly normal. While the growing season was all but over, multiple days of above normal temperatures, 10 to 20 degrees above normal, inhibited the ground from freezing. The drought remained unchanged through November as county-encompassing extreme drought (D3) extended from Mitchell and Floyd counties eastward to Winneshiek and Fayette counties. Widespread severe drought (D2) continued in Allamakee and Clayton counties with areas of extreme drought (D3).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought persisted across all of Mitchell County through November. The combination of total monthly precipitation near 0.25 inches, 2 inches below normal, and several days of anomalously warm temperatures resulted in 40 to 50% below normal topsoil moisture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-pressure system which had been moving slowly off the lee side of the Rocky Mountains had finally progressed into central Nebraska. This left western Iowa in the warm sector as a warm front moved north into the area. The SPC issued an Enhanced Risk for severe weather with a 30% hatched for severe hail. The severe weather mostly stayed west of the Missouri River. In Iowa, there was one report for a severe wind gust. The most impactful weather was a line of training storms that lead to flash flooding in Harrison County that caused significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Forest Service reported up to two dozen culverts washed out and six county roads that were partially or fully washed out. Large trees were washed into fields and there was significant damage to farm fields in several areas in the counties. There are significant areas of crop fields that likely lost in excess of 100 tons per acre from that one heavy rain event. The county estimated the damage to be approximately $400,000-$600,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave traveling across the region, paired with modest MUCAPE and moisture above the midlevels allowed for sub-severe to severe hail across portions of Northern Iowa through the late evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 1 SW Lu Verne and is time estimated. Hail shattered the windshield of the car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought maintained its presence across central Iowa during October despite some spotty rainfall.  The longer term dryness allowed any rain to soak in quickly with little response to area streams or rivers.  Severe to Extreme Drought persisted although some improvement was seen in northwest Iowa where precipitation for the month was over 200 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditons maintained a presence in Winnebago county during the month of October.  Temperatures remained above normal during the month with near normal rainfall although longer range precipitation remains well below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While parts of the 2023 summer had warm to hot temperatures, it took until middle to late August for the combination of hot temperatures and higher humidity levels to build a heat wave. Excessive heat advisories and warnings were initially issued on Monday, August 21st for select counties, expanding for all counties in northeast Iowa until Thursday, August 24th. Heat indices reached and exceeded 100 degrees each day, with overall highest heat indices occurring on Wednesday, August 23rd. During this time, maximum daytime heat indices reached 119 degrees at Decorah (Winneshiek County) and 118 degrees at Charles City (Floyd County) and Oelwein (Fayette County). Many sites in northeast Iowa recorded heat indices of 110 to 120 degrees over the four days from August 21st to August 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of anomalously high temperatures and humidity resulted in maximum heat indices of 116 degrees on Tuesday, August 22nd and 118 degrees on Wednesday, August 23rd at the automated weather observation station in Charles City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of anomalously high temperatures and humidity resulted in maximum heat indices of 108 degrees on Tuesday, August 22nd and 118 degrees on Wednesday, August 23rd in New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms impacted parts of northeast Iowa during the afternoon and into the evening of June 24th. Besides heavy rain, with 1.67 inches recorded in New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and 2.10 inches recorded near Elkport (Clayton County), damaging winds and hail also affected the area. Reports included tree damage in Oelwein (Fayette County) and 1 inch hail near Westgate (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1 tree down and 1 large branch down in Oelwein.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of rainfall and warmer than average temperatures resulted in severe drought conditions developing in northeast Iowa through June. Between April 1st and severe drought development by June 27th, approximately 6 inches of total rain fell in parts of Allamakee County, which was near 7 inches below normal for the time period or 25 to 50 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in Severe Drought for 387 square miles or 59% of Allamakee County towards the end of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the start of August, over 75% of northeast Iowa remained below severe drought (D2) conditions due to scattered storms in July. Similar scattered storms during the first half of August provided 1 to 2 inches of rain across northeast Iowa, preserving the ongoing moderate to severe drought areas. A lack of rainfall during the second half of August, less than an inch for most of northeast Iowa, was 1 to 3 inches below normal for the time period and expanded severe drought conditions across 90% of northeast Iowa by the middle of the month. By the end of August, all of northeast Iowa experienced at least severe drought (D2) conditions with 80% experiencing extreme drought (D3) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in extreme drought (D3) for all of Allamakee County through August. During the first half of August, approximately 1.5 inches of rain fell across Allamakee County, preventing any local severe drought impacts. Under a third of an inch of rain fell during the second half of August, 5 to 25 percent of normal for the time period, resulting in extreme drought (D3) for the entire county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the start of August, over 75% of northeast Iowa remained below severe drought (D2) conditions due to scattered storms in July. Similar scattered storms during the first half of August provided 1 to 2 inches of rain across northeast Iowa, preserving the ongoing moderate to severe drought areas. A lack of rainfall during the second half of August, less than an inch for most of northeast Iowa, was 1 to 3 inches below normal for the time period and expanded severe drought conditions across 90% of northeast Iowa by the middle of the month. By the end of August, all of northeast Iowa experienced at least severe drought (D2) conditions with 80% experiencing extreme drought (D3) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in extreme drought (D3) for all of Winneshiek County through August. During the first half of August, nearly an inch of rain fell across Winneshiek County, initially preventing any spread of very limited severe drought conditions. Under a half an inch of rain fell during the second half of August, 5 to 25 percent of normal for the time period, resulting in extreme drought (D3) for all of Winneshiek County. The United States Department of Agriculture declared Winneshiek County as a contiguous natural disaster area due to the drought on August 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions from late Spring through Summer resulted in approximately 30 percent of northeast Iowa counties experiencing severe drought (D2) conditions at the start of August. Monthly rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were 1 to 4 inches below normal, or 10 to 50 percent below normal. By the end of August, all of northeast Iowa from Mitchell and Floyd counties to Clayton county were in at least severe drought (D2), with approximately 85 percent of this area experiencing extreme drought (D3). Across the state, drought impacts were felt with reports of dry topsoil, increased crop disease, early harvest, and reports of livestock death loss.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in extreme drought (D3) for nearly all of Floyd County through August. During the first half of August, 1 to 2 inches of rain fell, initially preventing any spread of severe drought (D2) conditions. Under a half an inch of rain fell during the second half of August, 10 to 25 percent of normal for the time period, resulting in extreme drought (D3) towards the end of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of rainfall and warmer than average temperatures resulted in severe drought conditions developing in northeast Iowa during the middle of June. Between April 1st and severe drought development in Clayton, Chickasaw, Fayette and Floyd counties by June 20th, approximately 4 to 10 inches of total rain had fallen, which was 2 to 10 inches below normal for the time period, or 25 to 75 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in severe drought conditions for 164 square miles or 33% of Chickasaw County through the end of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions from late Spring through Summer resulted in approximately 30 percent of northeast Iowa counties experiencing severe drought (D2) conditions at the start of August. Monthly rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were 1 to 4 inches below normal, or 10 to 50 percent below normal. By the end of August, all of northeast Iowa from Mitchell and Floyd counties to Clayton county were in at least severe drought (D2), with approximately 85 percent of this area experiencing extreme drought (D3). Across the state, drought impacts were felt with reports of dry topsoil, increased crop disease, early harvest, and reports of livestock death loss.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 7 consecutive weeks severe drought (D2), worsened to extreme drought (D3) for the entire county through August. During the first half of August, half to 1.5 inches of rain fell across Allamakee County, initially preventing any spread of severe drought conditions. Only a tenth to half inch of rain fell during the second half of August, 5 to 25 percent of normal for the time period, resulting in extreme drought (D3). The United States Department of Agriculture declared Allamakee County as a contiguous natural disaster area due to the drought on August 24th.

During the first half of August, a 1 to 2 inches of rain fell, initially preventing any spread of severe drought (D2) conditions. Only a tenth to half an inch of rain fell during the second half of August, 5 to 25 percent of normal for the time period, resulting in extreme drought (D3) towards the end of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While parts of the 2023 summer had warm to hot temperatures, it took until middle to late August for the combination of hot temperatures and higher humidity levels to build a heat wave. Excessive heat advisories and warnings were initially issued on Monday, August 21st for select counties, expanding for all counties in northeast Iowa until Thursday, August 24th. Heat indices reached and exceeded 100 degrees each day, with overall highest heat indices occurring on Wednesday, August 23rd. During this time, maximum daytime heat indices reached 119 degrees at Decorah (Winneshiek County) and 118 degrees at Charles City (Floyd County) and Oelwein (Fayette County). Many sites in northeast Iowa recorded heat indices of 110 to 120 degrees over the four days from August 21st to August 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of anomalously high temperatures and humidity resulted in maximum heat indices reaching 112 degrees on Tuesday, August 22nd in Saratoga and 116 degrees on Wednesday, August 23rd in Riceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of rainfall and warmer than average temperatures resulted in severe drought conditions developing in northeast Iowa during the middle of June. Between April 1st and severe drought development in Clayton, Chickasaw, Fayette and Floyd counties by June 20th, approximately 4 to 10 inches of total rain had fallen, which was 2 to 10 inches below normal for the time period, or 25 to 75 percent of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in severe drought conditions for 574 square miles or 78% of Fayette County through the end of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms impacted northeast Iowa from the afternoon of June 6th into the evening hours. One storm produced severe 1 inch hail near Alpha (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 1 inch hail near Alpha via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions from late Spring through Summer resulted in approximately 30 percent of northeast Iowa counties experiencing severe drought (D2) conditions at the start of August. Monthly rainfall totals of 1 to 2 inches were 1 to 4 inches below normal, or 10 to 50 percent below normal. By the end of August, all of northeast Iowa from Mitchell and Floyd counties to Clayton county were in at least severe drought (D2), with approximately 85 percent of this area experiencing extreme drought (D3). Across the state, drought impacts were felt with reports of dry topsoil, increased crop disease, early harvest, and reports of livestock death loss.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 7 consecutive weeks of severe drought (D2), continued and worsened for all of Clayton County through August. During the first half of August, a half to 2 inches of rain fell, initially preventing any spread of severe drought (D2) conditions. During the second half of August, a tenth to 1 inch rain fell, 5 to 50 percent of normal for the time period, resulting in extreme drought (D3) by the end of August.

During the first half of August, half to 1.5 inches of rain fell, initially preventing any spread of severe drought (D2) conditions. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms impacted parts of northeast Iowa during the afternoon and into the evening of June 24th. Besides heavy rain, with 1.67 inches recorded in New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and 2.10 inches recorded near Elkport (Clayton County), damaging winds and hail also affected the area. Reports included tree damage in Oelwein (Fayette County) and 1 inch hail near Westgate (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 1 inch hail near Westgate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the start of August, over 75% of northeast Iowa remained below severe drought (D2) conditions due to scattered storms in July. Similar scattered storms during the first half of August provided 1 to 2 inches of rain across northeast Iowa, preserving the ongoing moderate to severe drought areas. A lack of rainfall during the second half of August, less than an inch for most of northeast Iowa, was 1 to 3 inches below normal for the time period and expanded severe drought conditions across 90% of northeast Iowa by the middle of the month. By the end of August, all of northeast Iowa experienced at least severe drought (D2) conditions with 80% experiencing extreme drought (D3) conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions resulted in extreme drought (D3) impacting all of Mitchell County through August. During the first half of August, approximately 1.5 inches of rain fell preventing any initial spread of ongoing very sparse severe drought impacts. Under a half inch of rain fell during the second half of August, 5 to 25 percent of normal for the time period, resulting in extreme drought (D3) for 445 square miles or 94% of Mitchell County.
